author: fiber_node
content:
INTERVIEW Q: Your React app renders a list of 10,000 items. The user types in a search box to filter the list. Every keystroke causes a visible lag — the input feels frozen.

The interviewer says: "Don't reach for virtualization. How does React's rendering model cause this, and what primitives does React give you to fix it?"

comments.author: state_mgr
comments.content: The input and the list share the same state update. React batches them into one render — but rendering 10K filtered items blocks the main thread, which blocks the input. Fix: split into two state values. The input text is urgent (needs immediate feedback), the filter query is deferrable. Use `useTransition` or `useDeferredValue` to mark the list re-render as low priority. React yields back to the browser between renders so the input stays responsive.

comments.author: fiber_node
comments.content: This is literally what Fiber was built for. The old stack reconciler couldn't pause mid-render — it was all or nothing. Fiber broke rendering into units of work that can be interrupted. `useTransition` is the developer-facing API for that interruptibility. The architecture IS the answer.

comments.author: use_effect
comments.content: And please don't say "debounce the input." That's a UX band-aid. The user still waits — they just wait after they stop typing instead of while they type. Concurrent features let React do BOTH: update the input instantly and render the list when there's time.

author: use_effect
content:
hot take: 80% of useEffect calls in production React apps shouldn't be useEffects.

fetching data? that's not a side effect, that's a render requirement. use a data fetching library or Server Components.

deriving state from props? that's not a side effect, that's a computation. do it during render.

syncing with an external store? useSyncExternalStore exists.

useEffect is for synchronizing React with things OUTSIDE React. If both sides of your sync are inside React, you're doing it wrong.

link.label: the real rules of useEffect in Lesson 4 →

comments.author: server_comp
comments.content: Fetching data in useEffect means: render empty shell → show spinner → fetch → re-render with data. That's three renders to show one page. Server Components: fetch on the server, stream the HTML. One render. Zero spinners. Zero waterfalls. The future is less useEffect, not better useEffect.

comments.author: state_mgr
comments.content: The React docs literally have a page called "You Might Not Need an Effect" and it covers like 12 patterns people use useEffect for that have better alternatives. If the React team is telling you to use their own hook less, maybe listen.

author: state_mgr
content: SPOT THE BUG: This component is supposed to show a count that increments every second. It renders "1" and never changes. No errors in console. What's wrong?

link.label: stale closures in hooks in Lesson 3 →

comments.author: use_effect
comments.content: Stale closure! The empty dependency array `[]` means the effect runs once on mount. The `count` inside the interval callback is captured as `0` forever. Every second it computes `0 + 1 = 1`. Fix: use the functional updater `setCount(c => c + 1)`. The updater always receives the current state, no closure needed.

comments.author: fiber_node
comments.content: This is the #1 hooks bug in the wild. I've seen senior engineers spend hours on it. The mental model shift: in hooks, every render has its own props, state, and effects. The interval lives in render #1's world forever unless you give it an escape hatch (functional updates, refs).

author: fiber_node
content:
MENTAL MODEL: React Fiber is a git branch for your UI.

When state changes, React doesn't modify the current UI directly. It creates a "work-in-progress" tree — a draft. It walks through components, calculates what changed, builds the new tree in memory.

Only when the entire tree is ready does React "commit" — swap the draft into the real DOM in one synchronous batch.

render phase = working on a branch (can be interrupted, restarted, abandoned)
commit phase = merging to main (synchronous, can't be interrupted)

That's why you can't do side effects during render — your "branch" might get thrown away. Effects run after commit, when you know the merge actually happened.

comments.author: suspenseful
comments.content: And Suspense is like a branch that's waiting for a CI check (data loading) before it can be merged. The fallback UI is shown while the branch is pending. When the data arrives, the branch is ready to commit. Same model, async data.

comments.author: use_effect
comments.content: This also explains StrictMode's double-render in dev. React is testing if your render is pure by rendering it twice and checking if the results match. If your render has side effects, the two renders will differ. It's a linter for purity, built into the runtime.

author: server_comp
content:
PSA: In the Next.js App Router, components are Server Components by default. You only add 'use client' when you need interactivity (state, effects, event handlers, browser APIs).

Stop putting 'use client' at the top of every file. You're opting OUT of the biggest React performance improvement in years because you're treating it like a boilerplate line.

If your component just fetches data and renders HTML, it should be a Server Component. Full stop.

link.label: Server Components from scratch in Lesson 10 →

comments.author: state_mgr
comments.content: The mental shift: 'use client' is not a file annotation, it's a BOUNDARY. Everything below that boundary is client. Everything above is server by default. Push the boundary as far down the tree as possible. Your page-level layout fetching data? Server. Your tiny interactive widget? Client. Minimize the client surface area.

comments.author: use_effect
comments.content: No useState, no useEffect, no event handlers, no browser APIs → Server Component. That checklist covers 80% of decisions. Stop overthinking it.

author: state_mgr
content: State management in 2025. What's your primary tool for global/shared state in a new React project?

comments.author: state_mgr
comments.content: The 6% who said "server state only" are ahead of the curve. Most "global state" is actually server state cached on the client. URL params, auth tokens, and maybe a theme toggle — that's your real client state. Everything else belongs in the data layer.

comments.author: fiber_node
comments.content: Zustand at 38% makes sense. It's 1KB, no Provider wrapper, works outside React, and the API is literally: create a store, use the store. That's it. The best state management is the one with the smallest API surface.

comments.author: server_comp
comments.content: If you're using Server Components properly, the amount of client state you need drops dramatically. The server handles data. The client handles interactions. Stop syncing what the server already knows.

author: suspenseful
content:
TIL: React.lazy() and Suspense aren't just for code splitting. You can use Suspense boundaries to show granular loading states for different parts of the page independently.

Wrap your sidebar in one Suspense, your main content in another, your comments in a third. Each resolves independently. The page loads progressively instead of all-or-nothing.

comments.author: server_comp
comments.content: This is even more powerful with Server Components + streaming. Each Suspense boundary becomes a streaming boundary. The server sends HTML for each section as soon as it's ready. Nav resolves in 50ms? Ship it. Comments take 2 seconds? The user is already reading the content. Progressive rendering at the protocol level.

comments.author: fiber_node
comments.content: The key insight: Suspense is not a loading spinner component. It's a declarative way to describe your UI's loading architecture. Where you place Suspense boundaries is an architectural decision, not a UI decision.

author: use_effect
content: SPOT THE BUG: This component fetches user data when the userId prop changes. It works... until you quickly switch between users. Then it shows the wrong user's data. No race condition errors — just wrong data silently displayed.

comments.author: state_mgr
comments.content: Classic race condition. Click user A → fetch starts. Click user B → new fetch starts. User B's response arrives first → shows B. User A's response arrives late → OVERWRITES with A's data. You're now looking at user A's profile while the URL says user B. Fix: use the cleanup function to set a `cancelled` flag, or use AbortController to cancel the stale fetch.

comments.author: server_comp
comments.content: Or... don't fetch in useEffect at all. Use a data library (TanStack Query, SWR) that handles race conditions, caching, and deduplication for you. Or better yet, make it a Server Component and fetch on the server where there IS no race condition because the response is the render.

comments.author: use_effect

author: server_comp
content:
THREAD: "Server Components are just PHP" is the most surface-level take in modern frontend.

PHP: server renders HTML, sends it, done. Client interaction = full page reload.

Server Components: server renders a SERIALIZABLE component tree, streams it to the client, the client MERGES it with existing client state. No page reload. State preserved. Instant navigation.

The server output isn't HTML — it's a description of the UI that React can reconcile. That's the key difference. PHP gives you a document. RSC gives you a diffable tree.

PHP: "here's a new page."
RSC: "here's what changed."

Same server. Completely different architecture.

comments.author: fiber_node
comments.content: The reconciliation point is crucial. When you navigate to a new Server Component route, React doesn't blow away the DOM. It diffs the new server tree against the current client tree. Your form input keeps its value. Your animation keeps playing. Your scroll position stays. PHP could never.

comments.author: suspenseful
comments.content: And streaming. PHP sends one complete response. RSC streams chunks wrapped in Suspense boundaries. The browser starts rendering before the server is done thinking. It's not request-response, it's request-stream. The architecture is fundamentally different from PHP, HTMX, Turbo, all of it.

author: state_mgr
content:
hot take: if you're prop drilling through more than 3 levels, the problem isn't that you need global state — the problem is your component tree is wrong.

composition > context > global state.

move the state closer to where it's used. pass components as children instead of data as props. restructure before you add a dependency.

redux for a theme toggle is like hiring a moving company to rearrange your desk.

comments.author: use_effect
comments.content: The children pattern is SO underused. Instead of `<Layout user={user}><Sidebar user={user}><Avatar user={user} /></Sidebar></Layout>`, just do `<Layout><Sidebar><Avatar user={user} /></Sidebar></Layout>`. The intermediate components don't need to know about user at all. Prop drilling solved, zero libraries.

comments.author: fiber_node
comments.content: Composition also helps performance. When you pass components as children, the parent re-rendering doesn't re-render the children (they're the same reference). It's accidentally optimal. Good architecture and good performance aligned — that's rare.

comments.author: server_comp
comments.content: "redux for a theme toggle" I see you've worked at my last company.

author: suspenseful
content:
React is becoming too complicated. We should go back to jQuery.

(ratio: 8K quote tweets)

comments.author: fiber_node
comments.content: jQuery: manually track which DOM nodes need updating. Manually clean up event listeners. Manually sync state between 47 different parts of the page. Manually handle routing. "Simple" until your app has more than one page and two developers. React's complexity exists because UI complexity exists. The complexity didn't go away — it moved from your code to the framework.

comments.author: state_mgr
comments.content: You don't miss jQuery. You miss the simplicity of the apps you built with jQuery. Those apps had 3 pages, one developer, and zero requirements for offline support, optimistic updates, or real-time data. Build that same app today and React is simple too.

comments.author: use_effect
comments.content: I literally spent a week debugging a jQuery app where two plugins both modified the same DOM node and neither knew about the other. The DOM was a shared mutable global and every plugin was writing to it. React's entire model — declarative rendering from state — exists to prevent exactly that nightmare. Never again.

author: use_effect
content:
git log --oneline:

c3d4e5f fix: removed useEffect that was setting state derived from props. it's called math
b7a8c9d refactor: moved fetch from useEffect to Server Component. deleted 40 lines of loading/error state
1e2f3a4 fix: added missing dependency to useEffect array. the lint rule was right. the lint rule is always right
5f6a7b8 feat: wrapped slow dashboard in Suspense boundary. users can read the header while charts load
9c0d1e2 chore: removed unused Context provider that wrapped the entire app for a single boolean

comments.author: server_comp
comments.content: "deleted 40 lines of loading/error state" — this is the Server Components pitch in one commit message. The best code is deleted code.

comments.author: state_mgr
comments.content: "removed unused Context provider that wrapped the entire app for a single boolean" I feel so seen right now. We've all been there. The boolean was probably `isDarkMode`.

author: fiber_node
content:
ELI5: What is reconciliation?

Imagine you have a LEGO house. Your mom says "make the roof red instead of blue." You have two choices:

1. Smash the entire house and rebuild it from scratch with a red roof.
2. Just swap the blue roof pieces for red ones.

React does option 2. When your state changes, React compares the old UI description with the new one and figures out the minimum number of LEGO pieces to swap. That comparison process is called reconciliation.

The virtual DOM is just the instruction sheet for what the LEGO house should look like. React diffs two instruction sheets and only touches the pieces that changed.

comments.author: suspenseful
comments.content: And the `key` prop is the serial number on each LEGO piece. Without a key, React matches pieces by position — "third brick from the left." With a key, React matches by identity — "the brick labeled 'roof-A'." That's why list keys matter: they help React know which piece is which when pieces move around.

comments.author: use_effect
comments.content: This is also why you shouldn't use `index` as a key for dynamic lists. If you remove the second item, the third item slides into position 2 and React thinks it's the SAME piece with different paint. It patches instead of replacing. Wrong piece, wrong update, wrong UI.

author: suspenseful
content:
MENTAL MODEL: Think of React rendering like a spreadsheet.

State values are the input cells. Components are formulas. The rendered UI is the output cells.

When you change an input cell (setState), the spreadsheet recalculates all formulas that depend on it (re-renders affected components) and updates the output (DOM).

You don't imperatively tell the spreadsheet WHICH cells to update. You just change the input and the spreadsheet figures it out. That's declarative UI.

useMemo is marking a formula cell as "only recalculate if these specific inputs changed." React.memo is marking an entire row as "skip this row if its inputs are the same." Optimization in React is just telling the spreadsheet where it can be lazy.

comments.author: state_mgr
comments.content: This also explains why immutability matters. A spreadsheet detects changes by comparing old value vs new value. If you mutate an object, the reference is the same — the spreadsheet thinks nothing changed and skips the recalculation. New reference = "this input changed, recalculate." That's the whole reason we spread objects instead of mutating.

comments.author: fiber_node
comments.content: And just like a spreadsheet can be slow if every cell depends on every other cell, a React app is slow if every component reads global state. Minimize dependencies. The best optimization is a component that depends on less state, same as the best formula references fewer cells.

author: server_comp

comments.author: fiber_node
comments.content: 340KB → 4KB by moving the render boundary. This is not an optimization technique. This is fixing an architectural mistake. Server Components don't make your app faster — they make it the speed it should have been all along.

comments.author: use_effect
comments.content: "Intl.NumberFormat in a useEffect" — I've seen this exact pattern in three different codebases. Price formatting is NOT a side effect. It's a computation. It goes in the render. Or better yet, on the server. Stop putting everything in useEffect. I will die on this hill.

author: fiber_node
content: SPOT THE BUG: This list renders fine at first, but when you sort it, the input values stay in the wrong rows. The labels move, but the inputs don't follow their items. No errors.

link.label: key prop and reconciliation in Lesson 8 →

comments.author: use_effect
comments.content: key={index} is the villain here. When you sort, item C moves from index 2 to index 0. React sees key={0} and thinks it's the SAME element — just with different text. So it patches the label but reuses the existing DOM input (with the old value). Fix: key={todo.id}. Keys are identity, not position.

comments.author: suspenseful
comments.content: This is the bug that makes every React interviewer smile. It looks correct, works on static lists, and silently breaks on reorder, filter, or sort. The rule: if your list can change order, never use index as key. And your list CAN change order — you just haven't added that feature yet.

comments.author: fiber_node
comments.content: Uncontrolled inputs make this even sneakier. defaultValue only sets the DOM on mount — after that, the DOM owns the value. When React reuses the DOM node (because key didn't change), it keeps the stale DOM value. Controlled inputs at least re-render with the right data. Uncontrolled + index keys = guaranteed bugs.

author: server_comp
content:
INTERVIEW Q: Your team is migrating a Next.js Pages Router app to the App Router. A page currently uses getServerSideProps to fetch data and passes it as props to a client-side component tree.

The interviewer asks: "Walk me through how you'd restructure this page to take advantage of Server Components. What stays on the client? What moves to the server? How do you handle the component that needs both fetched data AND onClick handlers?"

comments.author: server_comp
comments.content: Step 1: The page component itself becomes an async Server Component. It fetches data directly — no getServerSideProps, no API route, no useEffect. Step 2: Walk the tree. Any component that ONLY renders data? Server Component. Any component with state/effects/handlers? 'use client'. Step 3: The tricky one — a component that needs BOTH? Split it. Server Component fetches and renders the static parts, passes data as props to a small Client Component that handles the interaction. The boundary is the import.

comments.author: state_mgr
comments.content: The part people miss: you can pass Server Components as children to Client Components. The server renders them, the client just places them. So your <Accordion> can be a Client Component (handles open/close state) that renders server-fetched content as children. You don't need to choose server OR client — you compose them.

comments.author: use_effect
comments.content: And the best part of the migration: you delete code. getServerSideProps gone. Loading states for initial data gone. Error boundaries for fetch failures simplified. The component that was 200 lines of fetch-loading-error-render becomes 30 lines of async/await and JSX.

author: use_effect
content:
Stop using useEffect to sync state with props. You don't need an effect. You don't need state. You need a variable.

If the computation depends on props or state, just compute it during render. It runs every render anyway — that's WHEN you want it.

link.label: You Might Not Need an Effect in Lesson 4 →

comments.author: fiber_node
comments.content: The useEffect version is also SLOWER. Render 1: empty array shown. Effect fires, sets state. Render 2: filtered array shown. Two renders to compute one value. The direct computation version does it in one render. The "correct" code is also the faster code. Funny how that works.

comments.author: state_mgr
comments.content: I call this the 'state echo' anti-pattern. You're creating a state variable that just echoes what the props already tell you. Props change → effect fires → state updates → re-render. You've added an entire tick of delay and a wasted render to do what a const could do.

author: fiber_node
content:
hot take: React.memo() is a code smell.

not because memoization is bad — because if you need React.memo everywhere, your component architecture is wrong.

the parent is re-rendering too broadly. the state lives too high. the child receives too many props.

fix the structure. then you won't need the band-aid.

React.memo is duct tape for leaky re-renders. composition is the actual plumbing fix.

comments.author: state_mgr
comments.content: Agree and I'll go further: if you're wrapping 10 components in React.memo, ask yourself why the parent renders so often. Usually it's a Context that provides too much, or state that should be local is lifted too high. Move the state down, break the Context into smaller pieces, and suddenly React.memo is unnecessary.

comments.author: use_effect
comments.content: The exception: components rendered inside .map() over large datasets. That's a legitimate use case. But even then, if each item is its own component with its own state, React's reconciliation with proper keys already handles it well. Profile first, memo second.

comments.author: server_comp
comments.content: Server Components just skip this entire conversation. They don't re-render. There is no memo needed for a component that runs once on the server. The future is not better memoization — it's less client rendering.

author: state_mgr
content:
THREAD: Why Context makes everything re-render and what to do about it.

1/ React Context has a dirty secret: when the context value changes, EVERY component that calls useContext(ThatContext) re-renders. Not just the ones that use the part that changed. ALL of them.

2/ Put user, theme, locale, and cart in one Context? Changing the cart count re-renders every component that reads the user's name. They're completely unrelated but they share a Provider.

3/ The fix isn't React.memo (that's a band-aid). The fix is splitting your Context. UserContext, ThemeContext, CartContext. Each changes independently. Consumers only re-render when THEIR context changes.

4/ Or skip Context entirely for complex state. Zustand, Jotai, and other external stores let components subscribe to SLICES of state. Only the components reading `cart.count` re-render when the count changes. Surgical updates.

5/ Rule of thumb: Context is great for low-frequency, read-heavy data (theme, locale, auth). It's terrible for high-frequency, write-heavy data (form state, real-time data, frequently changing counts).

comments.author: fiber_node
comments.content: The reason this happens is architectural: Context uses a subscription model where the Provider is the publisher and every useContext is a subscriber. There's no diffing of the value — if the reference changes, everyone gets notified. Object.is(oldValue, newValue) is the only check. New object reference = everyone re-renders.

comments.author: use_effect
comments.content: I've seen teams put their ENTIRE Redux store into Context after removing Redux. Congratulations, you replaced Redux (which has selectors and prevents unnecessary re-renders) with Context (which doesn't). You downgraded your architecture and removed a dependency. Not the same thing.

comments.author: suspenseful
comments.content: Hot tip: if you MUST use a single Context for a complex value, wrap the value in useMemo and the children in React.memo. But at that point you're fighting the abstraction. Just use Zustand. It's 1KB and the selector pattern is built in.

author: server_comp
content:
MENTAL MODEL: The serialization boundary.

Server Components and Client Components live in different worlds. They communicate through a border — the serialization boundary.

Everything that crosses from server to client must be serializable: strings, numbers, booleans, arrays, plain objects, Date, Map, Set, and JSX (React elements).

What CAN'T cross: functions, classes, DOM nodes, closures. You can't pass an onClick handler from a Server Component because functions can't be serialized to JSON.

That's why 'use client' is a boundary marker, not a mode toggle. It says: "everything imported below this line lives on the client. Props passed to me must be serializable."

Think of it like an international border. You can send mail (data) across it, but you can't send a person (function) through the mail system.

comments.author: fiber_node
comments.content: The one exception that trips everyone up: you CAN pass a Server Action (an async function marked with 'use server') across the boundary. React serializes it as a reference — the client calls it, the request hits the server, the function runs there. It looks like passing a function, but it's actually an RPC call in disguise.

comments.author: state_mgr
comments.content: This is also why you can pass a Server Component AS A CHILD to a Client Component. The server renders the RSC into a serializable tree (React elements), and that tree crosses the boundary just fine. The Client Component doesn't even know its children came from the server. Props are data. Children are data. Functions are not data.

author: state_mgr
content: SPOT THE BUG: This form submits fine, but the user's edits disappear after submission. The form resets to the original values even though the save was successful. No errors.

comments.author: server_comp
comments.content: router.refresh() re-fetches the Server Component tree. The parent Server Component fetches the user again and passes the NEW user prop. But useState(user.name) only uses the initial value — it ignores prop changes after mount. The state is stale, showing the old initial value even though the prop updated. Fix: either add a key={user.updatedAt} to the form component to force remount, or use useEffect to sync the prop to state (one of the rare valid uses).

comments.author: use_effect
comments.content: This is the "initializer trap." useState's argument is the INITIAL value, not a binding. After mount, the prop and the state are decoupled. The prop changes, the state doesn't. If you want the state to follow the prop, you need to explicitly handle it. Or better yet, rethink whether you need local state at all — maybe the server is the source of truth.

comments.author: fiber_node
comments.content: The key prop trick is underrated here. <EditProfile key={user.id + user.updatedAt} user={user} />. When the key changes, React unmounts the old instance and mounts a new one. Fresh state. Clean slate. No sync logic needed. It's using reconciliation as a feature, not fighting it.

author: use_effect
content:
TIL: useReducer isn't just for complex state. It's a state machine disguised as a hook.

Instead of 5 related useState calls that you update together and sometimes get out of sync, one useReducer with named actions. The impossible states become impossible.

"loading but also error but also has data" can't happen when your reducer doesn't allow that transition.

comments.author: state_mgr
comments.content: This is the state machine insight that changed how I code. Don't model state as independent booleans — model it as a finite set of states with defined transitions. loading/success/error aren't three booleans. They're three states. A reducer enforces that you're always in exactly one of them.

comments.author: fiber_node
comments.content: Bonus: useReducer dispatch is stable across renders. Unlike useState's setter (also stable) combined with multiple states where you need useCallback for handlers, a single dispatch means one reference that never changes. Pass dispatch to child components and they never re-render from reference changes.

author: server_comp
content: React framework for a new project in 2025. Your team has 5 engineers, the app needs auth, DB access, and SEO. What do you pick?

comments.author: server_comp
comments.content: Next.js at 52% isn't surprising — it's the default for a reason. App Router + Server Components + Server Actions give you a full-stack framework that's genuinely good. The caching is complex, but the DX for data fetching is unmatched. You can query your DB in a component.

comments.author: state_mgr
comments.content: The 11% choosing SPA + separate API: I respect the simplicity, but you're signing up for building auth, SSR, routing, code splitting, and data fetching from scratch. You'll spend 3 months building what Next.js gives you on day one. Ship the product, not the framework.

comments.author: suspenseful
comments.content: Astro at 12% is the dark horse. If your app is mostly content with a few interactive islands, Astro ships zero JS by default and hydrates only what needs it. SEO is perfect because it's all HTML. But if the whole page is interactive, you're fighting the architecture.

author: use_effect
content:
hot take: the React Compiler (React Forget) will make half of Twitter's React performance advice obsolete.

no more "wrap this in useMemo." the compiler does it.
no more "useCallback for every handler." the compiler does it.
no more "React.memo your list items." the compiler does it.

the compiler auto-memoizes everything. your hand-rolled memoization was always a leaky abstraction anyway — you forget a dependency, it breaks silently.

machines are better at memoization than humans. let the compiler do its job.

link.label: React Compiler deep dive in Lesson 9 →

comments.author: fiber_node
comments.content: The compiler doesn't just add useMemo everywhere. It analyzes the data flow, finds values that are referentially stable, and skips re-computation at a granularity humans can't manage. It memoizes at the expression level, not the component level. Way more precise than anything you'd write by hand.

comments.author: state_mgr
comments.content: The catch: the compiler requires your components to follow the Rules of React. Pure render functions, no side effects during render, stable hook ordering. If your code is already clean, the compiler is free performance. If your code violates the rules, it can't help you. Good habits pay compounding returns.

comments.author: suspenseful
comments.content: This is the same trajectory as garbage collection. People used to manually manage memory and bragged about it. Then GC happened and the "manual is better" crowd slowly went silent. Memoization is the same — let the machine handle it.

author: suspenseful
content:
ELI5: What is a React Server Component?

Imagine ordering at a restaurant. The old way (client-side rendering): the waiter brings you all the raw ingredients and a recipe. You cook the meal yourself at the table. Takes forever. Your table (browser) is doing all the work.

Server Components: the kitchen (server) cooks the meal and the waiter brings you the finished plate. Your table does zero cooking. You just eat.

'use client' is you telling the waiter: "Actually, I want to grill this steak myself at the table." You only do that for the parts where you need to interact with the food (cut it, season it to taste). Not for the salad that's already done.

The goal: cook as much as possible in the kitchen. Only send raw ingredients for the parts the customer needs to interact with.

comments.author: server_comp
comments.content: And the really cool part: the kitchen can start sending plates as soon as each dish is ready (streaming). The appetizer arrives while the entree is still cooking. You don't wait for the entire meal to be done before you can start eating. That's Suspense + streaming SSR.

comments.author: fiber_node
comments.content: The restaurant analogy also explains why you can't use useState in a Server Component — the kitchen doesn't keep track of how much salt YOU added to your plate. Interactive state lives at the table (client). The kitchen just sends finished dishes.

author: state_mgr
content:
PSA: Zustand selectors are not optional. They're the entire point.

If you write `const state = useStore()` you subscribe to the ENTIRE store. Any change to any field re-renders your component.

`const count = useStore(s => s.count)` subscribes to ONLY count. Other fields change? Your component doesn't care. No re-render.

Selectors are Zustand's React.memo equivalent. They're how you get surgical updates without wrapping everything in memo.

comments.author: fiber_node
comments.content: Under the hood, Zustand uses useSyncExternalStore with a selector equality check. When the store updates, it runs your selector, compares the result with Object.is(), and only triggers a re-render if it changed. It's the same mechanism React uses internally. The selector IS the optimization.

comments.author: use_effect
comments.content: For derived values, the shallow equality helper is clutch: `const { name, email } = useStore(s => ({ name: s.name, email: s.email }), shallow)`. Without shallow, a new object is created every time and Object.is fails. With shallow, it compares each property. Details matter.

author: use_effect
content: SPOT THE BUG: This custom hook fetches data and provides a retry function. The retry works once, then stops working. No errors. The fetch just doesn't fire on subsequent retries.

comments.author: state_mgr
comments.content: retryCount isn't in the dependency array! The effect only re-runs when `url` changes. Clicking retry updates retryCount state (causing a re-render) but the effect ignores it because it's not a dependency. Fix: add retryCount to the dependency array `[url, retryCount]`. The effect re-runs whenever retry increments the count.

comments.author: fiber_node
comments.content: The exhaustive-deps lint rule would catch this instantly. It would warn: 'retryCount is not in the dependency array.' People disable this rule and then wonder why their effects don't work. The lint rule is not a suggestion. It's a correctness check.

comments.author: server_comp
comments.content: Or just use TanStack Query which gives you retry with exponential backoff, caching, deduplication, and background refetching for free. Every custom useFetch hook eventually becomes a worse version of React Query. I've seen it happen four times at four companies.

author: use_effect
content:
MENTAL MODEL: The useEffect dependency array is not "when to run." It's "what this effect synchronizes with."

Don't think: "I want this effect to run when X changes."
Think: "This effect needs to stay in sync with X."

The mental shift matters because it changes what you PUT in the array. You don't cherry-pick dependencies to control timing — you list every reactive value the effect reads. The effect runs as a consequence of those values changing, not because you told it to.

If your effect reads `count` but you leave it out of the array to "prevent it from running too often," you have a stale closure. Your effect is reading yesterday's newspaper and acting on it.

The dependency array is a declaration of truth, not a configuration option.

comments.author: fiber_node
comments.content: Dan Abramov's phrasing was perfect: useEffect is a synchronization tool. You synchronize React with some external system. The dependencies describe what that external system depends on. If the dependency changes, the synchronization needs to re-run. It's not a lifecycle hook — it's a sync primitive.

comments.author: state_mgr
comments.content: This is why the empty array `[]` doesn't mean "run once." It means "this effect synchronizes with nothing" — there's nothing it needs to stay in sync with. The run-once behavior is a consequence, not the definition. If you need to read state inside a [] effect, you probably need that state in the array.

author: fiber_node

comments.author: use_effect
comments.content: "I read a blog post" is the root cause of 90% of premature optimization. The React DevTools Profiler exists. It shows you EXACTLY which components render, how long they take, and why they re-rendered. Use it before adding a single memo. The data will surprise you — the slow component is never the one you think.

comments.author: server_comp
comments.content: style={{ color: 'red' }} in JSX is my personal nemesis. Every render creates a new object. Every React.memo check sees a "new" prop. The fix: const redStyle = { color: 'red' } outside the component, or a CSS class. Inline style objects are memo killers.

author: suspenseful
content:
INTERVIEW Q: You have a form where users can add and remove items from a list. When testing, you notice that removing the second item causes the third item's state to show the second item's data. The component uses index as key.

The interviewer asks: "Explain exactly what React does during reconciliation with index keys vs stable IDs. Walk through the DOM operations step by step for both approaches."

comments.author: fiber_node
comments.content:
With index keys: items = [A, B, C]. Remove B → [A, C]. React sees key=0 (was A, still A ✓), key=1 (was B, now C — patches B's DOM with C's props), key=2 (was C, now gone — removes it). Result: A stays, B's DOM gets C's text but keeps B's state (inputs, focus), and the old C gets deleted. Wrong item removed.

With stable keys: items = [A(id:1), B(id:2), C(id:3)]. Remove B → [A, C]. React sees key=1 (A, same ✓), key=2 (B, missing — remove it), key=3 (C, same ✓). Correct item removed. C keeps its own state.

comments.author: use_effect
comments.content: The key detail: React matches by key FIRST, then compares the element. With index keys, the keys shift, so React matches the wrong elements. With stable keys, each element always maps to the same DOM node regardless of position. Keys are identity. Indexes are positions. They are NOT the same thing.

author: server_comp
content:
Next.js Server Actions are not just for forms. They're any async function that runs on the server, callable from the client.

Use them for mutations, revalidation, even one-off server logic you don't want to build an API route for.

comments.author: use_effect
comments.content: Server Actions replaced three things I used to build for every mutation: 1) an API route, 2) a fetch call in the client, 3) a loading/error state machine in useEffect. Now it's one function. The import IS the API. The boundary IS the network. Zero boilerplate.

comments.author: state_mgr
comments.content: The revalidatePath/revalidateTag part is the real magic. After the mutation, Next.js automatically refetches the affected Server Components. The UI updates without you manually invalidating caches or refetching queries. The server mutates, the server re-renders, the client gets the fresh UI. Full circle.

comments.author: fiber_node
comments.content: Under the hood, 'use server' creates an HTTP endpoint. The client-side import becomes a fetch call. TypeScript carries the types across the boundary. You get end-to-end type safety without writing API contracts. It's the best abstraction I've seen for client-server communication in React.

author: state_mgr
content:
Signals are better than React's state model. React should just adopt signals and admit hooks were a mistake.

(ratio: 6K quote tweets)

comments.author: fiber_node
comments.content: Signals give you fine-grained reactivity — only the exact DOM node that reads a signal re-renders. React re-renders entire component subtrees. Different tradeoff, not objectively better. Signals are faster for updates but harder to reason about in complex UIs. React's model is slower per update but the component boundary makes code predictable. Predictability scales. Micro-optimizations don't.

comments.author: use_effect
comments.content: Also, the React Compiler is closing the gap. It auto-memoizes at the expression level, approaching signal-like granularity but keeping the component model. React chose to solve the performance problem with a compiler, not a paradigm shift. Disagree with the approach if you want, but calling hooks a 'mistake' is revisionism.

comments.author: server_comp
comments.content: Signals optimize CLIENT re-renders. Server Components eliminate client renders entirely. I'd rather skip the render than make it faster. The future is less client code, not faster client code.

author: suspenseful
content:
Error boundaries are the try/catch of React.

When a component throws during render, React walks up the tree looking for the nearest error boundary. If it finds one, it renders the fallback. If it doesn't, the entire app crashes.

You should have AT LEAST:
1. A root error boundary (catches everything, shows "something went wrong")
2. Error boundaries around independent sections (sidebar crash shouldn't kill the main content)
3. Error boundaries around data-dependent components (bad API response shouldn't crash the page)

comments.author: server_comp
comments.content: In Next.js App Router, error boundaries are even simpler: just create an error.tsx file. It automatically wraps the route segment in an error boundary. No class component needed. error.tsx for errors, loading.tsx for Suspense, page.tsx for content. The file system IS your UI architecture.

comments.author: fiber_node
comments.content: Fun fact: error boundaries still have to be class components. There's no hook equivalent of componentDidCatch or getDerivedStateFromError. It's one of the last class component holdouts. Libraries like react-error-boundary wrap this in a nicer API, but under the hood, it's still a class.

author: fiber_node
content: SPOT THE BUG: This component should toggle dark mode. Clicking the button changes the state, but the className never updates. The UI stays in light mode forever.

link.label: why you need useState in Lesson 2 →

comments.author: use_effect
comments.content: No useState! Mutating a local variable doesn't trigger a re-render. React doesn't know anything changed. The variable flips to true, the console confirms it, but React never re-renders the component, so the className stays 'light' forever. Fix: const [isDark, setIsDark] = useState(false). useState is not optional — it's how you talk to React.

comments.author: state_mgr
comments.content: This trips up every beginner coming from vanilla JS. In vanilla, mutating a variable and updating the DOM are the same mental model. In React, state is a SIGNAL to re-render. No signal = no render = no UI change. The console.log is the cruelest part — it proves the variable changed, but the UI didn't follow.

comments.author: suspenseful
comments.content: Also, even if it DID re-render somehow, `let isDark = false` runs again every render, resetting it to false. Local variables don't persist between renders. useState does. That's the second thing it gives you: persistence across renders, not just re-render triggers.

author: server_comp
content:
THREAD: Next.js caching explained without the confusion.

1/ There are four caches. Yes, four. Here's what each one does and when it matters.

2/ Request Memoization: Same fetch call in multiple components during ONE render? Deduplicated automatically. Fetch user in Layout and in Page? One network request. Lasts for the duration of the server render only.

3/ Data Cache: fetch() results cached on the server ACROSS requests. User A and User B hit the same page? One fetch. Opt out with `{ cache: 'no-store' }` or `{ next: { revalidate: 0 } }`. Revalidate with `revalidateTag` or `revalidatePath`.

4/ Full Route Cache: The rendered HTML and RSC payload for static routes. Cached at BUILD TIME for static pages. Dynamic pages (cookies, headers, searchParams) skip this.

5/ Router Cache: Client-side cache of visited routes. Navigate to /about, go back to /home, go to /about again? Served from the client cache. Cleared on refresh or after a timeout.

6/ The confusion comes from these caches overlapping. A fetch can hit the Request Memo, miss the Data Cache, but the Route Cache has the whole page. Understanding WHICH cache applies is the real skill.

comments.author: state_mgr
comments.content: This is the thread I wish existed when I started with App Router. The number of times I thought my data wasn't updating because of one of these caches... You mutate data, the DB updates, but the page shows stale data. Is it the Data Cache? The Full Route Cache? The Router Cache? Debugging caching is now a core React skill.

comments.author: fiber_node
comments.content: The mental model I use: Data Cache = server memory. Full Route Cache = CDN. Router Cache = browser memory. Request Memoization = per-request dedup. Each has different invalidation strategies and lifetimes. Map the cache to where it physically lives and it makes more sense.

comments.author: use_effect
comments.content: Honestly, the Next.js team has acknowledged the caching was too aggressive by default. Recent versions have dialed it back. But understanding the model is still essential — you need to know WHEN your data is fresh and WHEN it's stale, or your users see ghosts.

author: server_comp
content:
git log --oneline:

a1b2c3d fix: added revalidateTag after mutation. the cache was showing stale data for 30 seconds
d4e5f6a refactor: moved interactive modal to Client Component, kept parent page as Server Component
7b8c9d0 fix: removed 'use client' from data display component. it was shipping 120KB of JS to show a table
e1f2a3b feat: added loading.tsx to dashboard route. no more white flash on navigation

comments.author: fiber_node
comments.content: "exposing DB credentials" — this is the nightmare scenario with the server/client boundary. If you forget 'use server', the function gets bundled into the client JavaScript. Your DB connection string, API keys, everything. One missing directive, one catastrophic security hole. Lint rules for this are not optional.

comments.author: use_effect
comments.content: "removed 'use client' from data display component. it was shipping 120KB of JS to show a table" — we need a VS Code extension that warns you when a 'use client' component has zero state, zero effects, and zero event handlers. If it's purely rendering data, it's a Server Component waiting to be freed.

comments.author: suspenseful
comments.content: "added loading.tsx to dashboard route. no more white flash" — one file, zero lines of code you need to maintain, instant UX improvement. The App Router's file conventions are criminally underused. loading.tsx, error.tsx, not-found.tsx. Use them.

author: use_effect
content:
INTERVIEW Q: Explain the difference between controlled and uncontrolled components in React. When would you choose one over the other?

Follow-up: "You have a form with 30 fields. A colleague suggests making every field controlled with useState. Another suggests using refs and reading values on submit. What are the tradeoffs?"

comments.author: state_mgr
comments.content:
Controlled: React owns the value via state. Every keystroke triggers setState → re-render. You have instant access to the value for validation, conditional UI, derived state. Cost: 30 fields = 30 state variables = re-render on every keystroke.

Uncontrolled: The DOM owns the value. You read it with a ref when needed (usually on submit). No re-renders during typing. Cost: you can't easily react to changes, validate inline, or sync fields.

The 30-field form? Neither extreme. Use React Hook Form — it uses refs internally (uncontrolled) but gives you a controlled API when you need it. Best of both worlds.

comments.author: fiber_node
comments.content: The performance difference is real. Controlled: 30 fields, type one character, one setState, one re-render of the entire form component (and all 30 field components unless they're memoized). Uncontrolled: type one character, the DOM handles it, zero React re-renders. For large forms, uncontrolled + read-on-submit is dramatically faster.

comments.author: use_effect
comments.content: My rule of thumb: if you need to react to the value as it changes (search filter, character counter, real-time validation), use controlled. If you only need the value at a specific moment (form submission), use uncontrolled. Most form fields fall into the second category.

author: fiber_node
content:
TIL: The key prop isn't just for lists. You can use it to RESET a component's state.

Change the key, React unmounts the old instance and mounts a new one. Fresh state. No useEffect cleanup dance.

Editing user profiles? <ProfileForm key={userId} user={user} />. Switch users, the form resets. No stale state bugs. No "sync state from props" effects. Just a key.

link.label: key prop beyond lists in Lesson 8 →

comments.author: use_effect
comments.content: This single pattern eliminates an entire category of useEffect calls — the "sync state from props" pattern. Every time I see useEffect(() => { setState(prop) }, [prop]) I think: "this component needs a key on its parent, not an effect inside." The parent controls when to reset. The component doesn't need to know.

comments.author: state_mgr
comments.content: The tradeoff: changing a key unmounts and remounts the entire subtree. If the component has expensive initialization (fetching data, setting up subscriptions), that cost is paid again. For simple forms, it's perfect. For complex widgets with their own data loading, use the sync pattern instead. Know the cost before you use the trick.

author: server_comp
content:
hot take: Next.js App Router parallel routes are the most underused feature in modern React.

you can render multiple pages in the same layout simultaneously. independent loading states. independent error boundaries. one URL, multiple independent data streams.

a dashboard with a main panel, a sidebar, and a modal — each is its own route segment. each loads independently. the sidebar crashing doesn't kill the main panel.

people build this with client-side state and conditional renders. the router already solved it.

comments.author: suspenseful
comments.content: Parallel routes + Suspense is chef's kiss. Each slot gets its own loading.tsx. The main content loads in 200ms and renders. The analytics sidebar takes 2 seconds but shows a skeleton. The user is already working while the slow data loads. No waterfall. No spinner blocking the whole page.

comments.author: fiber_node
comments.content: The intercepting routes pattern is even wilder. Click a photo in a grid → modal opens (intercepted route). Refresh the page → full photo page renders. Same URL, different presentation based on how you got there. Instagram-style UX with zero client-side routing logic. The framework handles the interception.

comments.author: state_mgr
comments.content: I built a dashboard with parallel routes last month. The layout has @main, @sidebar, and @modal slots. Each is an independent route segment with its own loading, error, and not-found states. I deleted 400 lines of client-side state management for panel visibility, loading coordination, and error isolation. The router replaced all of it.

author: suspenseful
content: SPOT THE BUG: This component renders a list with infinite scroll. It works on first load but when you navigate away and come back, it shows duplicate items. The more times you navigate back, the more duplicates appear.

comments.author: use_effect
comments.content: StrictMode double-render! In development, React runs effects twice to detect missing cleanup. The effect appends items, runs again, appends the same items. In production, it runs once, so you don't see it — until you navigate back and the component remounts. The effect runs again, appending page 1 items to the existing list. Fix: either reset items on mount, or use a data fetching library that handles caching and deduplication.

comments.author: fiber_node
comments.content: The deeper issue: this effect has no cleanup. When the component unmounts and remounts, the old data is gone (fresh useState([])) but the effect runs and appends to the fresh array. If you had a client-side cache (TanStack Query, SWR), the cached data would be reused on remount instead of re-fetching and appending. The real fix is proper data management, not effect cleanup gymnastics.

comments.author: state_mgr
comments.content: Every time someone builds infinite scroll from scratch with useState + useEffect, a data fetching library author sheds a tear. TanStack Query's useInfiniteQuery handles pagination, caching, deduplication, and refetching. One hook. No bugs. Please stop reinventing the wheel.

author: state_mgr
content:
MENTAL MODEL: State colocation.

Every piece of state has a natural home — the closest common ancestor of all the components that need it. That's where it should live.

Too low: sibling components can't share it. You prop-drill up to share it.
Too high: unrelated components re-render when it changes. You add memo to fix it.
Just right: only the components that care about it are in its subtree.

Before you reach for Context or Zustand, ask: "Can I just move this state one level down?" Usually yes. Local state is the fastest state — it causes the smallest re-render blast radius.

comments.author: fiber_node
comments.content: The gravity analogy: state naturally wants to fall to the lowest point. Lift it only when something below needs to share it. The effort to lift state should be proportional to how many components need it. One component? Local state. Two siblings? Parent state. Entire app? Context or store. But always start low.

comments.author: use_effect
comments.content: I reviewed a codebase where the search query for a single page was in Redux. The search input and the filtered list were the only consumers. Both were children of the same component. The state traveled: component → dispatch → Redux store → useSelector → component. It could have been useState in the parent. Round trip of zero.

comments.author: server_comp
comments.content: And with Server Components, the question becomes: does this state even need to exist on the client? URL search params can be your search query state. The server reads them, fetches data, renders the result. Zero client state, zero re-renders, works with the back button. URL is the original global state manager.

author: use_effect
content:
PSA: useSyncExternalStore exists and it's what you should use when syncing React with browser APIs.

Window size, online/offline status, media queries, localStorage — these are external stores. They have their own state. React needs to subscribe to their changes.

useEffect + addEventListener + setState is the old pattern. useSyncExternalStore is the correct one.

comments.author: fiber_node
comments.content: The 'tearing' problem is real in concurrent mode. With useEffect + setState, React can pause mid-render, the external value changes, and different parts of the UI read different values from the same source. useSyncExternalStore prevents this by guaranteeing a consistent snapshot during the entire render. It's not just cleaner API — it's a correctness guarantee.

comments.author: state_mgr
comments.content: The third argument (server snapshot) is the part everyone forgets. Without it, the hook throws during SSR because window doesn't exist. The server snapshot gives React a value to use during server rendering. It's also why this hook works cleanly with Server Components — the snapshot is the fallback.

author: use_effect
content: Most common useEffect mistake you've seen in production?

options.text: Fetching data without race condition handling
Empty array when deps are needed

comments.author: use_effect
comments.content: 28% for derived state makes me want to scream and celebrate at the same time. Scream because it's SO common. Celebrate because people are recognizing it as a mistake. If your useEffect sets state based on other state or props, you almost certainly don't need it. Compute during render. useMemo if expensive.

comments.author: fiber_node
comments.content: Missing dependencies at 34% is the silent killer. The code works... until it doesn't. Stale closures don't throw errors. They just use old values. You can go weeks before noticing the bug. And when you do, it's in production, it's intermittent, and it's been there since that PR you didn't review carefully enough.

comments.author: suspenseful
comments.content: No cleanup at 19% — the classic. addEventListener without removeEventListener. setInterval without clearInterval. WebSocket open without close. It works in dev because you only mount once. In production with fast navigation? Memory leak city. The cleanup function is not optional.

author: state_mgr
content:
ELI5: Why can't you call hooks inside if statements?

React doesn't store hook values by name. It stores them by ORDER.

First render: hook 1 = useState(0), hook 2 = useEffect(...), hook 3 = useState('').
React makes a list: [hook1, hook2, hook3].

Second render: React goes through the list in order. "Hook 1? Here's your state. Hook 2? Here's your effect. Hook 3? Here's your state."

Now imagine hook 2 is inside an if statement that's false on the second render. React goes: "Hook 1? Here's your state. Hook 2? Here's your state — wait, that was the effect." The list is out of sync. Everything after the skipped hook gets the WRONG data.

That's why hooks must be called in the same order every render. No ifs. No loops. No early returns before hooks. The order IS the identity.

comments.author: fiber_node
comments.content: Internally, React uses a linked list of hooks attached to each Fiber node. On each render, it walks the list sequentially. There's no key or name — just position. This is a deliberate design choice: it keeps the hook system simple and fast, at the cost of the "no conditionals" rule. The constraint enables the performance.

comments.author: use_effect
comments.content: The escape hatch: you CAN conditionally do things inside a hook, just not conditionally call the hook itself. `useEffect(() => { if (isActive) { ... } }, [isActive])` is fine. `if (isActive) { useEffect(...) }` is not. The hook always runs. The logic inside can branch.

author: fiber_node
content:
Stop creating components inside other components. It looks innocent but it kills performance.

Every render of the parent creates a new component definition. React sees a "new" component type, unmounts the old one, and mounts a fresh one. All state inside is lost. All DOM is recreated. Every. Single. Render.

comments.author: use_effect
comments.content: The symptom is insidious: inputs lose focus on every keystroke. Because React unmounts and remounts the child, the DOM input is destroyed and recreated. The cursor position, selection, and focus are lost. If typing in a search box causes it to blur after every character, look for components defined inside other components.

comments.author: state_mgr
comments.content: The reason: React uses referential identity to determine if a component type changed. `function Child() {}` inside a render creates a new function reference each time. React compares OldChild !== NewChild, concludes the component type changed, and unmounts/remounts. At module level, the reference is stable. Same function = same component = state preserved.

author: use_effect
content:
THREAD: Custom hooks are not about reuse. They're about abstraction.

1/ Everyone teaches custom hooks as "extract shared logic." That's true but it's the SECOND reason to create them. The first reason: hiding complexity behind a clean interface.

2/ Even if only ONE component uses the hook, extracting it makes the component easier to read. `const { data, error, retry } = useUserProfile(userId)` is infinitely clearer than 30 lines of useState + useEffect + error handling inline.

3/ Custom hooks are where your imperative, messy, real-world code lives. The component stays declarative and clean. The hook does the dirty work. Separation of concerns at the function level.

4/ Test them independently. Mock the hook in component tests, test the hook's logic with renderHook. Two simple tests instead of one complicated test.

5/ Name them well. useUserProfile, useDebounce, useLocalStorage, useMediaQuery. The name IS the documentation. If you can't name it, the abstraction isn't clear yet.

comments.author: state_mgr
comments.content: Point 2 is the one that changed my code. I used to think "only one component uses this, why extract it?" Because the component is 200 lines long and I can't see the forest for the trees. Extract three custom hooks and the component becomes 20 lines of JSX that reads like documentation. The hooks handle the how. The component describes the what.

comments.author: fiber_node
comments.content: The testing point is underrated. `renderHook(() => useDebounce(value, 500))` lets you test timing, edge cases, and cleanup without rendering any UI. Try testing a debounced input inside a component — you're fighting React Testing Library's async utilities, faking timers, and querying DOM. The hook test is pure logic. Way easier.

comments.author: server_comp
comments.content: Custom hooks also make the Server Component migration easier. If your data fetching is in a useDataHook, you can replace the hook's implementation to use Server Components while the consuming component's interface stays the same. The abstraction boundary protects you from implementation changes.

author: state_mgr
content: SPOT THE BUG: This counter component renders TWO buttons and a count. Clicking "+3" should add 3 to the count. It only adds 1.

link.label: batching and functional updates in Lesson 2 →

comments.author: use_effect
comments.content:
React batches state updates within event handlers. All three setCount calls happen in the same render cycle, all reading `count` as 0 (or whatever it was at the start). So it's: setCount(0+1), setCount(0+1), setCount(0+1). Three instructions all saying "set to 1." Result: 1.

Fix: use functional updates. setCount(c => c + 1) three times. Each updater receives the PENDING state, not the stale closure value. 0→1→2→3. Correct.

comments.author: fiber_node
comments.content: Batching is a feature, not a bug. Without batching, three setCount calls would cause three re-renders. With batching, React queues them and applies them in one render. The functional updater form is how you compose batched updates correctly — each update builds on the previous one instead of overwriting it.

author: suspenseful
content:
MENTAL MODEL: Suspense boundaries are loading architecture.

Place them based on what the USER should see while waiting, not where your data fetching happens.

Does the user need the nav to work while the content loads? Suspense boundary between nav and content.

Is the sidebar independent of the main panel? Separate Suspense boundaries.

Does a slow API call block the entire page? Wrap just that section.

Every Suspense boundary is a UX decision: "What's the smallest useful piece of UI I can show while the rest loads?" The answer shapes your component tree, not the other way around.

comments.author: server_comp
comments.content: In the App Router, each loading.tsx IS a Suspense boundary. So your route structure is your loading architecture. Nested routes = nested loading states. /dashboard/analytics has its own loading.tsx, independent of /dashboard. The routing tree maps directly to the loading UX tree. File system as architecture.

comments.author: fiber_node
comments.content: The mistake I see most: one Suspense boundary at the root, wrapping everything. That gives you an all-or-nothing loading state — the entire page shows a spinner until the slowest component finishes. You've recreated the pre-Suspense loading experience. More boundaries = more granular = better perceived performance.

author: state_mgr
content:
React Hook Form + Zod is the best form stack in React right now.

RHF handles the form state without re-renders (uses refs internally). Zod handles validation with TypeScript inference. Together: type-safe forms with zero re-render cost.

Define your schema once, get validation AND types. Your form inputs, error messages, and submit handler all share the same type. Change the schema, TypeScript errors show everywhere.

link.label: Zod + React Hook Form in Lesson 12 →

comments.author: server_comp
comments.content: The beauty of Zod schemas: they work on the server too. Use the same schema in your Server Action to validate the request body. Client validates for UX. Server validates for security. Same schema. Same types. One source of truth across the boundary.

comments.author: use_effect
comments.content: RHF's register pattern is genius. `{...register('email')}` spreads ref, onChange, onBlur, and name onto the input. No controlled state. No useEffect. No re-renders. The form just works and React barely knows it's there. Anti-React? No. Anti-unnecessary-re-render.

author: fiber_node
content:
INTERVIEW Q: Two components on the same page both need to display the current user's name. You could lift the state to a common parent, use Context, or use a global store.

The interviewer says: "Walk me through the re-render implications of each approach. When does the USERNAME changing cause components that DON'T display the username to re-render?"

comments.author: state_mgr
comments.content:
Lifted state: parent re-renders → ALL children re-render (unless memoized). Username changes → the parent, BOTH username components, AND every sibling component re-render. Blast radius is the entire parent subtree.

Context: username changes → context value changes → EVERY component calling useContext(UserContext) re-renders. Even if a component only reads user.theme and not user.name, it re-renders. Blast radius is every consumer.

External store (Zustand): `useStore(s => s.user.name)`. Username changes → ONLY components whose selector returns a different value re-render. Blast radius is surgical. That's why stores exist.

comments.author: fiber_node
comments.content: The key insight interviewers want: React has no dependency tracking within Context values. When the Context value object changes reference, ALL consumers re-render — React doesn't know which property each consumer reads. External stores CAN track dependencies because they control the subscription. Selectors + equality checks = fine-grained subscriptions. That's the fundamental architectural difference.

author: suspenseful
content:
hot take: loading spinners are a failure of architecture.

every spinner is a question: "why doesn't the user have this data yet?"

SSR? data is there on first paint. no spinner.
streaming? the shell renders instantly, content fills in. partial spinner at most.
prefetching? data is already cached before navigation. no spinner.
optimistic updates? UI updates before the server confirms. no spinner.

spinners are a confession that your data loading strategy is reactive instead of proactive.

the best loading state is one the user never sees.

comments.author: server_comp
comments.content: This is exactly the RSC pitch. The server fetches data BEFORE the client even renders. The HTML arrives with data already in it. No loading state needed for initial render. The spinner era was a client-side rendering era problem. We're moving past it.

comments.author: state_mgr
comments.content: The hierarchy: 1) Don't need to load (SSR/RSC). 2) Load before the user asks (prefetch). 3) Show stale while loading (SWR pattern). 4) Show skeleton (structural placeholder). 5) Show spinner (last resort). Each step down is a worse UX. Most apps jump straight to step 5.

comments.author: fiber_node
comments.content: Skeletons > spinners every time. A skeleton tells the user WHAT's coming and WHERE it will appear. A spinner says "something is happening, who knows what." Skeletons reduce layout shift. Spinners create anxiety. The perceived performance difference is dramatic even if the actual wait time is identical.

author: server_comp
content:
TIL: In React 19, the `use()` hook can read promises and context — and unlike other hooks, you CAN call it inside conditionals and loops.

It's not a traditional hook. It's more like an `await` for React. Pass it a promise, it suspends the component until the promise resolves, then returns the value.

comments.author: fiber_node
comments.content: The conditional thing blows people's minds but it makes sense: use() doesn't store state in a linked list like other hooks. It reads from the promise or context directly. No position-dependent storage = no ordering constraint. It's a fundamental shift in what a "hook" is.

comments.author: use_effect
comments.content: The pattern of passing promises as props is key. The Server Component creates the promise (starts the fetch), the Client Component consumes it with use(). The fetch starts on the server before the client even renders. No waterfall. No useEffect. The data is already in flight when the component mounts.

author: server_comp
content: SPOT THE BUG: This Server Action is supposed to create a new todo item. It works locally but in production, after creating a todo, the list doesn't update. The item IS in the database — it just doesn't appear until a full page refresh.

comments.author: state_mgr
comments.content: No revalidation! The Server Action creates the todo in the DB but doesn't tell Next.js to refetch the page data. The page is cached (Full Route Cache or Data Cache), so it serves the stale version. Fix: add `revalidatePath('/todos')` after the DB write. This tells Next.js: 'this page's data is stale, re-render it.'

comments.author: fiber_node
comments.content: This works locally because in development, Next.js doesn't cache as aggressively (or not at all in recent versions). Production has the full caching stack enabled. The gap between dev and prod behavior around caching is one of the biggest gotchas in Next.js. Always test with `next build && next start` before shipping.

comments.author: use_effect
comments.content: You can also use `revalidateTag('todos')` if you tagged your fetch: `fetch(url, { next: { tags: ['todos'] } })`. Tags are more surgical — they only invalidate fetches with that tag, not the entire path. For large pages with multiple data sources, tags are the way.

author: fiber_node
content:
hot take: you should learn React without a framework first.

not because frameworks are bad — Next.js is excellent. but because frameworks hide the why.

if you've never written a useEffect data fetch and handled the race condition yourself, you won't appreciate why TanStack Query exists.

if you've never fought Context re-renders, you won't understand why Zustand's selectors matter.

if you've never built client-side routing, you won't grok what Next.js does for you.

learn the pain. then learn the painkiller. the pain is the context that makes the solution click.

comments.author: server_comp
comments.content: Counterpoint: the React docs now recommend starting with a framework. And they're right — for shipping products. But I agree with the learning angle. If you've never seen the problem, the solution is magic. And magic doesn't transfer. Understanding transfers.

comments.author: use_effect
comments.content: The number of developers who use TanStack Query but can't explain what problem it solves is concerning. "It fetches data." No, fetch() fetches data. TanStack Query handles caching, deduplication, background refetching, race conditions, stale-while-revalidate, pagination, infinite queries, and optimistic updates. If you don't know what those are, you can't configure the library correctly.

comments.author: state_mgr
comments.content: Same energy as "learn vanilla JS before React." You don't need to be an expert, but you need to feel the friction. Spend a weekend building a small app with just React (Vite + React, no framework). Feel the routing pain. Feel the data fetching pain. Then add Next.js and watch the pain disappear. Now you understand the abstraction.

author: use_effect

comments.author: fiber_node
comments.content: 47 renders per keystroke on a mobile device with a throttled CPU. That's not a theoretical performance problem — that's a janky, laggy input that makes users abandon checkout. Forms are the one place where uncontrolled inputs (or ref-based libraries) objectively outperform controlled state. The data doesn't change until submit. Why render until submit?

comments.author: server_comp
comments.content: This is also why Server Actions + <form> is interesting. The form submits to the server. No client-side state at all. The browser handles the form natively. React only re-renders when the server responds with new UI. For simple forms, it's the ultimate zero-re-render solution.

author: suspenseful
content:
The loading.tsx file in Next.js App Router is not just a convenience — it's a streaming boundary.

When a route has a loading.tsx, Next.js wraps the page in a Suspense boundary automatically. The server streams the loading UI immediately, then streams the page content when it's ready.

No client-side JavaScript needed. No useEffect. No loading state. The server handles everything.

One file. Instant perceived performance.

comments.author: server_comp
comments.content: The beauty of the file convention: you don't have to understand Suspense to use it. Create loading.tsx, put a skeleton in it, done. Under the hood, it's <Suspense fallback={<Loading />}><Page /></Suspense>. The abstraction handles the plumbing. Junior devs get streaming SSR without knowing what streaming SSR is.

comments.author: fiber_node
comments.content: Nest them for granular loading states. /dashboard has a loading.tsx, /dashboard/analytics has its own loading.tsx. The parent dashboard shell streams first, then the analytics section fills in. Progressive rendering through the file system. It's nested Suspense boundaries without writing a single <Suspense>.

author: fiber_node
content:
MENTAL MODEL: Components are functions. Renders are function calls. Props are arguments.

Every time React re-renders a component, it calls the function again with new arguments (props + state). The function returns a description of what the UI should look like NOW.

The function doesn't modify the previous UI. It returns a new description. React diffs the descriptions and updates the DOM. The function is PURE — same inputs, same output. Side effects go in useEffect, which runs AFTER the function returns.

This is why you can't mutate state directly: the function already returned. It's like trying to change the arguments of a function that already ran. You need to call the function again with new arguments (setState → new render → new function call).

React components are not objects with lifecycle methods. They're pure functions that React calls when inputs change.

comments.author: use_effect
comments.content: This mental model also explains why hooks have their rules. If a component is a function and renders are calls, then hooks are registrations that happen during the call. They must happen in the same order every call so React can match them up. Early returns, conditionals, loops — they all break the ordering guarantee.

comments.author: state_mgr
comments.content: And this is why class components feel so different. Classes are objects — they persist between renders. The same instance is mutated. `this` changes. Lifecycle methods are called on the same object. Functions don't have `this`. Each call is independent. Each render is a snapshot. The functional model is simpler once you stop thinking in objects.

author: state_mgr
content:
INTERVIEW Q: Explain the difference between useCallback and useMemo. When should you use each? Give me a scenario where using useCallback is actually harmful.

Follow-up: "The React Compiler is coming. Does that change your answer?"

comments.author: fiber_node
comments.content:
useMemo memoizes a VALUE. useCallback memoizes a FUNCTION (which is a value, but the API is specialized for functions).

`useMemo(() => expensiveCalc(a, b), [a, b])` — caches the result of expensiveCalc.
`useCallback((x) => doSomething(x, a), [a])` — caches the function reference.

useCallback is harmful when the memoized function is passed to a non-memoized component. The child re-renders anyway (parent re-rendered), so the stable reference is wasted. You paid for the memoization (comparing deps) with zero benefit. useCallback without React.memo on the child is performance theater.

comments.author: use_effect
comments.content: With the React Compiler: you don't need either. The compiler auto-memoizes expressions and functions when it determines they would produce the same result. It's smarter than manual memoization because it operates at the expression level, not the hook level. Your useCallback with wrong deps? The compiler won't make that mistake.

comments.author: suspenseful
comments.content: My rule until the compiler ships: don't memoize unless you've profiled and found a real problem. The overhead of useMemo/useCallback (dependency array comparison every render) is small but nonzero. If the computation it replaces is also small, you're spending CPU to save CPU. Profile first. Optimize second.

author: server_comp
content:
PSA: You can pass Server Components as children to Client Components. This is the composition pattern that makes RSC flexible.

The Server Component renders on the server. Its output (serialized React elements) is passed as the children prop. The Client Component receives and places them. The client doesn't know or care that the children were server-rendered.

comments.author: fiber_node
comments.content: The reason this works: children are props, and props cross the serialization boundary as data. The Server Component renders to a React element tree (data), which is passed as the children prop (data) to the Client Component. The client receives pre-rendered elements, not a component to execute. No server code runs on the client.

comments.author: state_mgr
comments.content: This pattern is the answer to "how do I use server data in a client-interactive wrapper?" Don't make the wrapper a Server Component (it needs state). Don't make the content a Client Component (it doesn't need state). Compose them. Server content inside a client shell. Both do what they're good at.

author: use_effect
content: SPOT THE BUG: This component adds a scroll listener to show a "back to top" button. It works, but after navigating to another page and back, there are TWO scroll listeners running. Navigate back again: three. Memory keeps growing.

comments.author: fiber_node
comments.content:
No cleanup function! addEventListener runs on mount but there's no removeEventListener on unmount. Navigate away → component unmounts, listener stays. Navigate back → new mount, new listener. Now two handlers fire on every scroll event. Classic memory leak.

Fix: return a cleanup function that removes the listener. Also, store the handler in a variable so you remove the same function reference you added.

comments.author: state_mgr
