comments.content: The anonymous arrow function is the other problem. `window.addEventListener('scroll', () => {...})` — you can't remove an anonymous function because you have no reference to it. You MUST name the function: `const handleScroll = () => {...}`. Then `addEventListener('scroll', handleScroll)` and `return () => removeEventListener('scroll', handleScroll)`. The named reference is the cleanup hook.

comments.author: suspenseful
comments.content: Also missing: passive flag for scroll listeners. `{ passive: true }` tells the browser the handler won't call preventDefault(), so it can scroll smoothly without waiting for your handler. Tiny detail, big UX improvement on mobile. Performance death by a thousand scroll handlers.

author: fiber_node
content:
THREAD: Compound components — the pattern that makes component APIs beautiful.

1/ Instead of a component with 20 props, create a component with composable children. The parent provides context, the children consume it. The API reads like English.

2/ Compare: <Select options={options} value={value} onChange={onChange} placeholder="Pick one" disabled={false} clearable={true} searchable={true} maxHeight={300} />

Vs: <Select value={value} onChange={onChange}>
  <Select.Trigger>Pick one</Select.Trigger>
  <Select.Content>
    <Select.Search />
    {options.map(o => <Select.Item key={o} value={o} />)}
  </Select.Content>
</Select>

3/ The second version is more code but infinitely more flexible. Want a divider between options? Drop in <Select.Separator />. Want a custom render for one item? Wrap it. Want icons? Add them as children. The API is open for extension.

4/ Implementation: the parent provides a Context, children consume it. Select manages the open/close state, Item reads the selected value and calls onChange. The child components are coupled to the parent by convention, not by props.

5/ This is how Radix UI, Headless UI, and every good component library works. Learn this pattern.

comments.author: use_effect
comments.content: The Context-based implementation is key. The parent creates a context: { isOpen, selectedValue, onSelect }. Each child reads what it needs. Select.Trigger reads isOpen to toggle. Select.Item reads selectedValue to highlight itself and calls onSelect on click. No prop drilling. No render props. Just context and composition.

comments.author: state_mgr
comments.content: The design principle: maximize flexibility, minimize API surface. 20 props is a big API surface. 5 sub-components is a big flexibility surface. The compound pattern trades prop count for component count, and components compose better than props. You can wrap, extend, and rearrange components. You can't rearrange props.

comments.author: suspenseful
comments.content: Bonus: compound components are naturally accessible. Each sub-component can handle its own ARIA attributes. Select.Trigger gets role='combobox'. Select.Content gets role='listbox'. Select.Item gets role='option' + aria-selected. The accessibility is baked into the architecture, not bolted on with 5 more props.

author: state_mgr
content:
git log --oneline:

f1a2b3c fix: Zustand selector was returning new object every render. added shallow equality
4d5e6f7 refactor: split UserContext into AuthContext and PreferencesContext. re-renders dropped 60%
8a9b0c1 feat: replaced useState-based form with React Hook Form. checkout renders: 47 → 0 per keystroke
d2e3f4a fix: Context provider was inside a component that re-renders on route change. moved to layout
5c6d7e8 refactor: extracted usePermissions hook. 4 components had identical permission-checking logic

comments.author: fiber_node
comments.content: "selector was returning new object every render" — the most common Zustand mistake. `useStore(s => ({ name: s.name, email: s.email }))` creates a new object reference every time. Zustand uses Object.is() by default. New reference = not equal = re-render. The fix: `import { shallow } from 'zustand/shallow'` and pass it as the second argument. Or select each field individually.

comments.author: use_effect
comments.content: "Provider was inside a component that re-renders on route change" — this is a GREAT catch. If the Provider's parent re-renders, the Provider re-renders, creating a new value object, causing ALL consumers to re-render. Providers belong in layouts that don't re-render on navigation. Stable parent = stable provider = stable consumers.

author: state_mgr
content:
TIL: Jotai atoms are like useState but global and composable.

Define atoms anywhere. Use them in any component. Derived atoms auto-recompute when dependencies change — like a spreadsheet formula.

No store. No provider. No actions. No reducers. Just atoms.

comments.author: fiber_node
comments.content: The derived atom pattern is what makes Jotai special. In Zustand, you derive in the selector. In Context, you derive in the consumer. In Jotai, the derivation is in the ATOM definition. The computation is centralized, cached, and shared across all consumers. Change countAtom → doubleAtom recomputes → only components reading doubleAtom re-render.

comments.author: use_effect
comments.content: No Provider is the killer feature for me. Zustand also skips the Provider, but Jotai's atom-based approach means each piece of state is independently subscribable by design. You don't need selectors to avoid re-renders — each atom IS a selector. The granularity is built into the model.

author: fiber_node
content: React testing strategy. Where do you get the most confidence per test?

comments.author: use_effect
comments.content: Integration tests at 42% aligns perfectly with the Testing Library philosophy: test components the way users use them. Render the page, mock the API with MSW, interact with elements, assert on what the user sees. You test behavior, not implementation. Refactor the component internals, the test still passes.

comments.author: suspenseful
comments.content: E2E at 28% is the pragmatist's choice. Playwright running against your actual app catches everything: routing, data fetching, auth flows, CSS layout bugs. It's slow and flaky, but when it passes, you KNOW it works. One good E2E test replaces 20 unit tests in confidence.

comments.author: state_mgr
comments.content: TypeScript at 4% is underrated. A well-typed codebase eliminates entire categories of bugs: wrong prop types, missing required props, impossible state shapes. You don't need to test that a function receives a string when TypeScript won't compile if you pass a number. Types are tests that run at build time.

author: use_effect
content:
React Testing Library: stop using getByTestId. Use accessible queries instead.

Priority order: getByRole > getByLabelText > getByPlaceholderText > getByText > getByTestId.

getByRole('button', { name: 'Submit' }) tests that the element IS a button AND is labeled correctly. getByTestId('submit-btn') tests that a div has a data attribute. One verifies accessibility. The other doesn't.

comments.author: fiber_node
comments.content: The last point is the insight that makes Testing Library brilliant. If getByRole can't find your button, a screen reader can't find it either. The testing difficulty IS the accessibility audit. Fix the test by fixing the HTML. Use semantic elements. Add ARIA labels. The test and the UX improve together.

comments.author: suspenseful
comments.content: getByTestId has exactly one legitimate use: when the element has no accessible role and adding one would be semantically incorrect. Example: a styled container div that you need to assert is visible. Everything else should be queryable by role or label. If it's interactive, it has a role.

author: server_comp
content:
hot take: the "framework wars" are over. The answer is boring.

need SEO + dynamic data + full-stack? Next.js.
mostly content site with some interactivity? Astro.
already using React Router and happy? Remix.
build tool and nothing else? Vite + React.

there is no universal best. there are tradeoffs. pick the one whose tradeoffs match your constraints and move on.

the time you spend debating frameworks is time you're not shipping features. your users don't know or care which framework you chose. they care if the page loads fast and the button works.

comments.author: state_mgr
comments.content: "Your users don't know which framework you chose" should be on a poster in every frontend team's office. The most successful products I've worked on used "boring" technology chosen quickly. The worst products I've worked on were built on whatever was trending that month. Stability > novelty. Ship > debate.

comments.author: fiber_node
comments.content: The nuance people miss: these frameworks are converging. Next.js is adding Remix-like data patterns. Remix adopted React Server Components. Astro added server islands. They're all approaching the same destination from different starting points. The differences are shrinking. Pick one and commit.

comments.author: use_effect
comments.content: Also, switching frameworks mid-project is almost never worth it. The migration cost is always higher than expected, the team velocity drops for months, and the end result is... the same app on a different framework. Invest that energy in UX improvements instead. Users notice UX. Users don't notice framework migrations.

author: suspenseful
content: SPOT THE BUG: This component shows a notification toast that auto-dismisses after 3 seconds. But if the user clicks "dismiss" manually and another toast appears immediately, the new toast disappears instantly without waiting 3 seconds.

comments.author: fiber_node
comments.content:
The dependency array is empty `[]`, so the effect runs once on mount. But here's the problem: if the parent reuses the same Toast instance with a new message (changing the `message` prop but not unmounting), the timer from the OLD message is still running. The cleanup doesn't fire because the effect's dependencies didn't change. The old timer fires and dismisses the new toast early.

Fix: add `message` (or a unique toast ID) to the dependency array. New message → cleanup old timer → start new 3-second timer.

comments.author: use_effect
comments.content: This is also a stale closure on `onDismiss`. If the parent passes a new onDismiss function (which it probably does since it's likely an inline arrow), the effect still has the OLD onDismiss captured in its closure. Adding onDismiss to the deps would fix both issues, but might cause unnecessary timer resets. Best fix: `key={toast.id}` on the Toast component to force a fresh mount per toast.

comments.author: state_mgr
comments.content: The key approach is cleanest: <Toast key={currentToast.id} message={currentToast.message} onDismiss={dismiss} />. New toast ID = new component instance = fresh effect = fresh timer. No dependency array gymnastics. Let React's lifecycle handle the reset.

author: use_effect
content:
MENTAL MODEL: useEffect is a synchronization tool, not a lifecycle hook.

Old mental model (from classes): "componentDidMount → run setup." "componentDidUpdate → react to changes." "componentWillUnmount → clean up."

New mental model (hooks): "This effect synchronizes X with Y. When Y changes, re-synchronize. When the component is done with Y, clean up."

The lifecycle model thinks in TIME (mount, update, unmount). The hooks model thinks in DEPENDENCIES (what does this effect sync with?).

A chat room connection effect: "Synchronize the WebSocket connection with the current roomId. When roomId changes, disconnect from old room, connect to new room. When done, disconnect."

That's one effect that handles mount, update, AND unmount — because it's one synchronization, not three lifecycle events.

comments.author: fiber_node
comments.content: The shift from lifecycle thinking to synchronization thinking is the hardest part of learning hooks for class-component developers. In classes, you think: 'when does this happen?' In hooks, you think: 'what does this depend on?' The effect doesn't care WHEN it runs — it cares WHAT it synchronizes with. The timing is a consequence of the dependencies.

comments.author: server_comp
comments.content: And the ultimate synchronization insight: if you're synchronizing React state with React state (derived state), you don't need an effect at all. Effects are for synchronizing React with NON-React things: DOM APIs, timers, network, local storage, third-party libraries. If both sides are React, just compute during render.

author: fiber_node
content:
React DevTools Profiler: the tool you should be using but aren't.

Step 1: Click "Record." Step 2: Do the slow thing. Step 3: Click "Stop."

You'll see exactly which components rendered, how long each took, and WHY they re-rendered (state change, props change, parent render, context change).

Stop guessing where your app is slow. The Profiler knows. Ask it.

comments.author: use_effect
comments.content: The "Why did this render?" feature is worth installing the extension for alone. Click a component in the flame chart, it says: "Props changed: items" or "The parent component rendered." Now you know EXACTLY what to fix. Not guessing. Not adding React.memo everywhere. Surgical optimization based on data.

comments.author: state_mgr
comments.content: Enable "Record why each component rendered" in the Profiler settings (gear icon). It's off by default but it's the most useful option. Without it, you see WHAT rendered. With it, you see WHY. The difference between observing symptoms and diagnosing causes.

comments.author: suspenseful
comments.content: Pro tip: profile in a production build (`npm run build && npm start`). Dev mode includes extra checks (StrictMode double-renders, development warnings) that inflate render times. Your app is faster in production than the profiler shows in dev. Profile where it counts.

author: use_effect
content:
Server Components are overengineered. Most apps are SPAs that don't need SSR. Just use Vite + React and stop complicating everything.

(ratio: 5K quote tweets)

comments.author: server_comp
comments.content: "Most apps don't need SSR" — most apps also ship 2MB of JavaScript, show a white screen for 3 seconds on first load, have terrible SEO, and require a separate API layer. Server Components aren't about SSR. They're about running code where it makes sense: data fetching on the server, interactivity on the client. That's not overengineering. That's correct engineering.

comments.author: fiber_node
comments.content: The 'just use Vite + React' crowd underestimates how much plumbing they're signing up for. Routing, code splitting, data fetching, loading states, error handling, caching, SEO, auth, type-safe APIs... You'll either build a framework poorly or install 15 libraries. At that point, you ARE using a framework. Just a worse one.

comments.author: suspenseful
comments.content: SPA is fine for dashboards behind auth where SEO doesn't matter and users expect a loading screen. But calling that 'most apps' is wild. E-commerce? Needs SSR. Blog? Needs SSR. Landing page? Needs SSR. SaaS marketing site? Needs SSR. The SPA-only use case is smaller than people think.

author: server_comp
content:
useOptimistic is React 19's answer to "the UI feels laggy when saving."

Show the result immediately. Update the real state when the server confirms. If the server fails, roll back automatically.

The user sees instant feedback. The save happens in the background. The UX goes from "click... wait... done" to "click... done (while saving)."

comments.author: state_mgr
comments.content: The pending flag pattern is brilliant UX. Show the item at 50% opacity while saving. The user knows it's there, knows it's in progress, and can keep working. If it fails, it disappears (or shows an error). No modal. No toast. No blocking. Just seamless optimistic rendering.

comments.author: suspenseful
comments.content: useOptimistic + Server Actions is the combo that makes apps feel native. Click a like button → heart fills immediately (optimistic) → server saves in background. If it fails, the heart unfills. No spinner. No delay. Instagram-level responsiveness in a React Server Component architecture.

author: use_effect
content:
INTERVIEW Q: You have a modal component. When the modal is open, pressing Escape should close it. When the modal is closed, Escape should do nothing.

The interviewer says: "Write the useEffect for the keyboard listener. I want to see the cleanup function, the dependency array, and I want you to explain what happens if the modal opens and closes rapidly."

comments.author: use_effect
fiber_node
comments.content: The early return pattern is important: when isOpen is false, you return without adding a listener AND without returning a cleanup function. That means next time the effect runs (when isOpen becomes true), there's no stale listener to clean up. The effect is self-consistent: it only cleans up what it sets up.

comments.author: state_mgr
comments.content: The onClose in the dependency array is the part most people miss — and it matters. If onClose is an inline function in the parent, it's a new reference every render, and the effect re-runs every render (removing and re-adding the listener). Solutions: useCallback on onClose in the parent, or useEffectEvent (React 19 experimental) to read the latest onClose without it being a dependency.

author: server_comp
content:
ELI5: What is streaming SSR?

Normal SSR is like ordering a pizza. You call, wait 30 minutes, the ENTIRE pizza arrives. You eat.

Streaming SSR is like a buffet conveyor belt. Sushi arrives every 30 seconds. You start eating the first piece while more are being made. You don't wait for all 50 pieces to be ready.

The server doesn't wait until the entire page is rendered. It sends each section as soon as it's ready. The browser starts painting immediately.

The header arrives in 50ms — painted. The product info arrives in 200ms — painted. The reviews take 2 seconds — the user is already reading the product info while reviews stream in.

No blank white screen. No spinner blocking the page. Just content appearing progressively.

comments.author: suspenseful
comments.content: And the Suspense boundaries are where the sushi chef cuts between pieces. Each <Suspense> is a chunk boundary. The server streams each chunk independently. More Suspense boundaries = more granular streaming = faster perceived load time. The architecture of your component tree directly maps to the streaming behavior.

comments.author: fiber_node
comments.content: The technical trick: the server sends an HTML shell with placeholder divs for each Suspense boundary. As each section resolves, the server sends a <script> tag that replaces the placeholder with the real content AND hydrates it. The browser never does a full repaint — it swaps in content incrementally. It's HTML streaming with React-aware hydration.

author: state_mgr
content: SPOT THE BUG: This shopping cart uses Context for state. Adding an item to the cart causes the ENTIRE app to re-render — including the footer, nav, and every page component. Performance is terrible.

comments.author: fiber_node
comments.content: The value prop `{{ items, total, addItem }}` creates a new object on every render. Even if items and total haven't changed, the object reference is new. Every consumer of CartContext sees a "new" value and re-renders. Fix 1: useMemo the context value. Fix 2: separate the data (items, total) from the actions (addItem) into two contexts — actions never change, so action consumers never re-render.

comments.author: use_effect
comments.content: Even with useMemo, adding an item changes `items` (new array reference) AND `total` (new number). Components that only display the total still re-render because the context value changed. This is the fundamental Context limitation: no granular subscriptions. Every consumer gets the whole value. Split into CartItemsContext and CartTotalContext, or use Zustand with selectors.

comments.author: server_comp
comments.content: The children pattern also helps here. <CartProvider>{children}</CartProvider> means the children are the SAME React elements across re-renders (same reference, passed from above). The Provider re-renders but the children don't re-create. Only consumers that call useContext re-render. The children prop is a free optimization.

author: suspenseful
content:
THREAD: React 19's form APIs will change how we build forms.

1/ React 19 treats <form> as a first-class primitive. The action prop accepts a function — including async functions and Server Actions. No more onSubmit + preventDefault + manual state management.

2/ useFormStatus(): get the pending state of the nearest parent <form>. Is the form submitting? Show a spinner. Disable the button. No manual isPending state needed.

3/ useFormState(): manage form state + server validation in one hook. The server returns errors, the hook provides them to the form. Client and server validation unified.

4/ useOptimistic(): show the result immediately while the action runs. Add a todo → it appears instantly (optimistic) → server confirms → optimistic state replaced with real state.

5/ Together: <form action={serverAction}> + useFormStatus (pending UI) + useFormState (server errors) + useOptimistic (instant feedback). Full form lifecycle without useState, useEffect, or try/catch. The platform handles it.

comments.author: server_comp
comments.content: The server validation story is what excites me most. The Server Action runs on the server, validates with Zod, returns errors in a structured format. useFormState receives those errors on the client. No API route. No fetch. No try/catch. The form talks directly to the server validation. Same schema, both sides.

comments.author: state_mgr
comments.content: useFormStatus is a game-changer for submit buttons. Put it in a SubmitButton component, use it in every form. `const { pending } = useFormStatus(); return <button disabled={pending}>{pending ? 'Saving...' : 'Save'}</button>`. One component, works with every form. No prop drilling the pending state.

comments.author: use_effect
comments.content: This is the death of the 'form state management library' category for simple forms. React Hook Form is still great for complex client-side logic, but for a basic save-to-server form? React 19's built-in APIs do everything you need with zero dependencies. The framework absorbed the library.

author: fiber_node
content:
Code splitting with React.lazy() — stop shipping your entire app on first load.

Every route, every modal, every heavy component behind a conditional should be lazy-loaded. The user visiting /home shouldn't download the code for /settings.

React.lazy + Suspense is the simplest code splitting in any framework.

comments.author: suspenseful
comments.content: The prefetch-on-hover pattern is the UX secret weapon. The user hovers the nav link → the code starts downloading. By the time they click, the chunk is already cached. Zero loading spinner. It FEELS instant because you predicted the navigation 200ms before it happened.

comments.author: server_comp
comments.content: In Next.js, code splitting is automatic per route. Each page.tsx is a separate chunk. React.lazy is still useful for heavy components WITHIN a page — a chart library, a rich text editor, a PDF viewer. Don't ship pdfjs just because the user might click 'Export to PDF.'

author: state_mgr
content:
MENTAL MODEL: Render props are dead. Long live render props.

Everyone says "hooks replaced render props." Mostly true — for logic reuse. But render props are still the best pattern for component INVERSION OF CONTROL.

A render prop says: "I handle the behavior. YOU decide the appearance."

A headless component (Radix, Headless UI, Downshift) is a render prop in spirit — it handles state, accessibility, and keyboard interactions. You provide the markup. The library is behavior-only. Your design system is untouched.

Hooks extract logic. Render props/headless components extract behavior + give you rendering control. Different tools, both alive.

comments.author: fiber_node
comments.content: The modern version isn't literally `render={fn}` — it's the slot pattern. Radix's <Select.Trigger asChild> renders YOUR component but applies its behavior to it. Headless UI passes props via renderProps: `{({ open }) => <button>{open ? 'Close' : 'Open'}</button>}`. Same idea, better API. The render prop evolved, it didn't die.

comments.author: use_effect
comments.content: The distinction matters for library authors: if your library provides UI, it's opinionated and hard to style. If it provides behavior via hooks/render props, it's flexible and easy to integrate. The best React libraries separate behavior from presentation. That's the render prop legacy.

author: use_effect
content: SPOT THE BUG: This component uses useEffect to update the document title. It works, but in React 18 Strict Mode, the title flickers on mount — it briefly shows the old title, then the new one, then the old one again.

comments.author: fiber_node
comments.content:
Strict Mode runs effects twice: mount → cleanup → mount. First mount: title set to new value. Cleanup: title reset to OLD value (captured on first mount). Second mount: title set to new value again. The flicker is: new → old → new. The cleanup is restoring the wrong thing.

The real question: should this effect restore the old title at all? Probably not. If the component unmounts, the new page probably sets its own title. Remove the cleanup. Or capture the initial title once in a ref, not on every effect run.

comments.author: state_mgr
comments.content: This is why StrictMode exists — it caught a bug. The cleanup assumes the 'old title' at cleanup time is the one you should restore. But in concurrent mode, the component might mount, unmount, and remount rapidly. The old title captured at mount #1 might be stale by mount #2. The effect's cleanup is not symmetric with its setup.

comments.author: server_comp
comments.content:
In Next.js App Router, just use the `metadata` export or `generateMetadata()`. The server sets the title. No useEffect. No client-side DOM manipulation. No flicker.
```
export const metadata = { title: 'My Page' };
```
Done. Server-rendered. Correct on first paint.

author: suspenseful
content: Biggest React anti-pattern you still see in 2025 codebases?

options.text: index as key in dynamic lists

comments.author: use_effect
comments.content: useEffect for derived state at 32% is my brand and I'm honored. But 'use client' on every file at 26% is the one that costs the most real-world performance. You're shipping kilobytes — sometimes megabytes — of JavaScript for components that do zero interactive work. The fix is one delete keystroke.

comments.author: fiber_node
comments.content: God components at 15% — the 500-line file where one component handles data fetching, state management, event handlers, and rendering. You can't test it. You can't reuse parts of it. You can't even READ it. Break it up. Extract hooks for logic. Extract components for UI sections. The file should tell a story, not contain an encyclopedia.

comments.author: state_mgr
comments.content: Prop drilling at 18% is interesting because it's not always an anti-pattern. Two levels of prop passing is FINE. It's explicit, traceable, and has zero performance cost. The anti-pattern is drilling through 5-10 levels because you refuse to use Context or composition. The cure (global state everywhere) can be worse than the disease (a few extra props).

author: use_effect
content:
AbortController in useEffect is how you properly cancel async operations.

Forget the `let cancelled = false` pattern. AbortController actually cancels the network request — the browser stops waiting for the response. Less bandwidth. Less CPU. Cleaner code.

comments.author: fiber_node
comments.content: The `cancelled` boolean pattern only prevents setState after unmount — the fetch still completes, the response still downloads, the JSON still parses. You just ignore the result. AbortController actually aborts the TCP connection. The server stops processing (if it respects the signal). Real cancellation, not pretend cancellation.

comments.author: state_mgr
comments.content: The AbortError catch is the part people forget. When you abort a fetch, it throws an error. If you don't specifically filter out AbortError, your error handling treats a user clicking away as a crash. Always check err.name !== 'AbortError' before logging or showing error UI.

author: suspenseful

comments.author: use_effect
comments.content: The sequential await trap is SO common with Server Components. async/await syntax makes serial execution feel natural. You have to actively think 'are these independent?' and restructure with Promise.all. The syntax nudges you toward waterfalls. Fight the syntax.

comments.author: server_comp
comments.content: The Suspense boundary approach is even better than Promise.all because each section can STREAM independently. Promise.all waits for all three, then renders everything. Suspense boundaries render each section as it resolves. The user sees content 2 seconds sooner because you didn't wait for the slowest query.

author: state_mgr
content:
hot take: most React performance problems aren't React problems. they're architecture problems.

fetching data in a child component that the parent already has? waterfall.
rendering 10,000 items without virtualization? wrong tool.
putting everything in global state? every update re-renders everything.
creating inline objects in JSX? memo can't help you.

the framework is fast. your patterns are slow. profile. restructure. then optimize.

comments.author: fiber_node
comments.content: React renders a component in microseconds. The expensive parts are: DOM updates (minimize them), data processing during render (memoize it), and unnecessary re-renders (fix the architecture). React.memo addresses the third one, but the first two are your code, not React's. Profile before blaming the framework.

comments.author: use_effect
comments.content: The waterfall point is huge. Parent renders, fetches data, renders child, child fetches its own data. That's a sequential waterfall — the child's fetch can't start until the parent's fetch finishes. Hoist the child's fetch into the parent (or use parallel routes in Next.js). Parallel > sequential. Always.

comments.author: suspenseful
comments.content: The virtualization one is my favorite to debug. 'React is slow rendering our list.' How many items? '10,000.' Are you virtualizing? 'No.' Well, no framework is fast rendering 10,000 DOM nodes. Use react-window or react-virtuoso. Render 20 items at a time. React is fine. Your expectations aren't.

author: fiber_node
content:
TIL: You can use a key prop to force a CSS transition/animation to replay.

Change the key, React unmounts the old element and mounts a new one. The CSS animation starts fresh. No JavaScript animation library needed.

Perfect for notification banners, toast animations, or "shake on error" effects.

comments.author: suspenseful
comments.content: This is so elegant. The key prop isn't just for lists — it's React's reset button. CSS animations play on mount. Change the key = force a remount = replay the animation. You're using React's reconciliation as an animation trigger. Zero JS animation logic.

comments.author: use_effect
comments.content: Just be aware: changing the key destroys ALL DOM state in that subtree. Input values, scroll position, focus — all gone. For a simple animation div, that's fine. For a complex form that happens to shake on error, you probably want to toggle a class via state instead. Know what you're resetting.

author: state_mgr
content:
INTERVIEW Q: Your React app uses a WebSocket connection for real-time updates. The connection should open when the component mounts, reconnect on disconnect, and close on unmount.

The interviewer asks: "How do you manage this with hooks? What are the pitfalls around cleanup, stale closures, and reconnection logic?"

comments.author: use_effect
fiber_node
comments.content: In practice, extract this into a useWebSocket custom hook. The component shouldn't manage connection logic — it should call `const { messages, send } = useWebSocket(url)` and not think about cleanup, reconnection, or buffering. The hook hides the imperative mess. The component stays declarative.

comments.author: state_mgr
comments.content: StrictMode double-mount is the gotcha here. In dev, React mounts → unmounts → remounts. Your socket opens, closes, opens again. If your server tracks connections, you'll see two opens and one close for every mount. The double-mount is testing that your cleanup works correctly. If you get zombie connections in dev, your cleanup is broken.

author: server_comp
content:
Next.js generateMetadata() is the right way to do dynamic page titles and meta tags. It runs on the server, has access to params and data, and produces correct meta tags for SEO crawlers on first response.

No useEffect document.title hacks. No Helmet library. No client-side DOM manipulation.

comments.author: fiber_node
comments.content: The deduplication point is key. People worry about fetching the product twice (once in generateMetadata, once in the page). Don't. Next.js automatically deduplicates identical fetch calls within the same request. One DB query, two consumers. It's free.

comments.author: suspenseful
comments.content: The openGraph metadata means social media previews work correctly without any client-side rendering. Share a product link on Twitter, the image and description appear immediately. This is impossible with client-side document.title — social media crawlers don't execute JavaScript.

author: use_effect
content:
hot take: TypeScript is the best testing tool in the React ecosystem.

not because it replaces tests. because it eliminates entire categories of bugs that tests would otherwise catch.

wrong prop types? compile error.
missing required props? compile error.
impossible state shapes? discriminated unions.
forget to handle a case? exhaustive switch check.
return the wrong type from a hook? compile error.

TypeScript errors are tests that run on every keystroke, cost zero runtime, and never go stale. add tests for BEHAVIOR. let types handle STRUCTURE.

comments.author: state_mgr
comments.content: The discriminated union point is underrated. Instead of `{ isLoading: boolean, error: Error | null, data: T | null }` (8 possible states, most impossible), use `{ status: 'loading' } | { status: 'error', error: Error } | { status: 'success', data: T }`. TypeScript enforces that you can only access .error when status is 'error'. Impossible states are unrepresentable.

comments.author: fiber_node
comments.content: I stopped writing tests for prop validation after adopting TypeScript. I used to write: 'it should throw if name is not a string.' Now TypeScript won't LET you pass a non-string. The test is literally unwriteable because the bug is unwriteable. That's a stronger guarantee than any runtime test.

author: fiber_node
content: SPOT THE BUG: This component memoizes a filtered list to avoid recalculating on every render. But the memoization never works — the filter runs every render anyway, just as if useMemo wasn't there.

comments.author: use_effect
comments.content:
The dependency array RUNS the filter to create the dependency value! `users.filter(u => u.role === role)` in the deps creates a new array every render. useMemo compares the new array to the old one with Object.is(). New array reference !== old array reference. Memo invalidated. The filtering you're trying to avoid is happening INSIDE the dependency check.

Fix: `[users, role]`. These are the actual inputs to the computation. If users and role haven't changed, the filtered result hasn't changed.

comments.author: state_mgr
comments.content: This is why the dependency array should contain the INPUTS, not a re-computation of the OUTPUT. The whole point of useMemo is: 'if these inputs are the same, skip the computation.' If you put the computation in the dependency check, you're doing the work twice: once to check, once to compute. Worse than no memoization.

author: server_comp
content:
THREAD: RSC vs SSR — they are not the same thing. Stop confusing them.

1/ SSR (Server-Side Rendering): Render React components to HTML on the server. Send HTML to client. Client downloads JavaScript. Client "hydrates" — attaches event listeners to the existing HTML. Result: fast first paint, but you ship ALL the component JavaScript.

2/ RSC (React Server Components): Components that run ONLY on the server. Their code never ships to the client. No hydration needed. The output is a serialized React tree, not HTML. The client receives the tree and renders it.

3/ SSR is about WHEN you render (server first, then client). RSC is about WHERE you render (some components server-only, some client-only). They solve different problems.

4/ You can have RSC WITHOUT SSR (client renders Server Component output). You can have SSR WITHOUT RSC (Next.js Pages Router). You can have BOTH (Next.js App Router). They're independent axes.

5/ The big difference: SSR still ships component JavaScript for hydration. RSC ships ZERO JavaScript for server components. A Server Component with 50 imports, 200 lines of logic, and a heavy markdown parser? None of that code reaches the client. That's the bundle size win SSR can't provide.

comments.author: fiber_node
comments.content: Point 4 is what most people miss. SSR and RSC are orthogonal concepts. SSR: 'render on the server for a fast first paint.' RSC: 'run this component on the server because it doesn't need client interactivity.' You can combine them, but they're solving different problems. SSR optimizes initial load. RSC optimizes bundle size and data access.

comments.author: suspenseful
comments.content: The hydration difference is the key performance insight. SSR sends HTML + JavaScript → client downloads JS → hydration makes it interactive. RSC sends rendered output + NO JavaScript for server components. The client only hydrates the Client Components. Less JS to download = faster TTI. It's not just a DX improvement — it's a fundamental performance architecture change.

author: fiber_node
content:
git log --oneline:

a3b4c5d fix: moved component definition outside render function. inputs stopped losing focus on every keystroke
2e3f4a5 perf: replaced 10,000-item list with react-virtuoso. scroll is smooth. memory dropped 80%
6b7c8d9 fix: added AbortController to useEffect fetch. navigating fast no longer causes setState on unmounted component
e0f1a2b refactor: split 600-line UserDashboard into 8 focused components. each is under 80 lines
3c4d5e6 feat: compound component API for <DataTable>. 20 props → 5 composable sub-components

comments.author: use_effect
comments.content: "moved component definition outside render function. inputs stopped losing focus" — if I had a dollar for every time this was the fix, I'd retire. The component-inside-component pattern is the most expensive mistake in React. It looks harmless. It destroys all child state on every parent render. Move it to module level. Always.

comments.author: state_mgr
comments.content: "split 600-line UserDashboard into 8 focused components. each is under 80 lines" — this is the commit that makes the next 100 commits easier. Debugging an 80-line component takes minutes. Debugging a 600-line component takes hours. The split isn't just clean code — it's a velocity multiplier.

comments.author: suspenseful
comments.content: "compound component API for DataTable. 20 props → 5 composable sub-components" — chef's kiss. <DataTable.Column>, <DataTable.Pagination>, <DataTable.Search>. The API reads like documentation. Add a feature? Add a sub-component. Remove a feature? Remove it. No prop soup.

author: state_mgr
content:
URL state is the most underused state management tool in React.

Search query? URL search param. Filters? URL search params. Sort order? URL search param. Current tab? URL search param.

Benefits: shareable (copy the URL, share the exact view), bookmarkable, back-button works, SSR-compatible, zero client-side state to manage.

Stop putting UI state in useState when it belongs in the URL.

comments.author: server_comp
comments.content: And with Server Components, URL params are even more powerful. The server reads the params, fetches filtered/sorted data, and renders it. The URL IS the query. No client-side state, no useEffect fetch, no loading spinner for initial render. The server does the work, the URL is the API, the HTML is the result.

comments.author: use_effect
comments.content: The nuxt/sveltekit approach of making search params reactive is one of the things React frameworks should steal. In Next.js, reading params is easy but updating them is verbose (create URLSearchParams, set values, router.push). Libraries like nuqs make this much cleaner with React hooks that read/write URL params like useState.

author: server_comp
content:
MENTAL MODEL: The React tree is a spectrum from server to client.

At the top: Server Components. Static. Data-heavy. Zero JS shipped.

In the middle: the 'use client' boundary. The serialization border.

At the bottom: Client Components. Interactive. State. Event handlers.

Your job is to push the boundary as LOW as possible. The more of your tree that's server-rendered, the less JavaScript your users download.

Page layout? Server. Data display? Server. Navigation? Server. The tiny like button? Client. The search input? Client. The modal toggle? Client.

Maximize the server surface. Minimize the client surface. That's the RSC architecture in one sentence.

comments.author: fiber_node
comments.content: The spectrum metaphor is better than the boundary metaphor because it implies gradual transition. You don't have "server pages" and "client pages" — you have a tree where each component individually decides. A single page can have 90% server components and three tiny client interactive bits. The granularity is per-component, not per-page.

comments.author: state_mgr
comments.content: The practical trick: start by making everything a Server Component. Then add 'use client' only when the compiler yells at you for using useState, useEffect, or an event handler. Let the errors guide you. The default is server. The exception is client. This ensures you never accidentally ship server-only code to the client.

author: suspenseful
content: SPOT THE BUG: This loading skeleton works great... except in Next.js App Router, where the page content flashes briefly before the skeleton appears. The skeleton shows AFTER the content, not before. Completely backwards.

comments.author: server_comp
comments.content: The Suspense boundary wraps the ENTIRE layout, including the sidebar. But the page is the only slow part. When the page data loads, the layout un-suspends and shows everything at once — including the skeleton flash before React hydrates. Fix: use loading.tsx (which wraps only the page slot) or move the Suspense to wrap only `{children}` and keep <Sidebar /> outside it.

comments.author: fiber_node
comments.content: The deeper issue: Suspense boundary placement is an architectural decision. Wrapping too much means unrelated fast content waits for slow content. Wrapping too little means no loading state at all. The sweet spot: wrap each independently-loading section in its own Suspense. The sidebar loads fast? Don't make it wait for the dashboard data.

comments.author: use_effect
comments.content: Just use loading.tsx. That's literally what it's for. It automatically wraps the page content (not the layout) in a Suspense boundary. The layout renders immediately, the page shows a skeleton until data arrives. No manual Suspense placement needed. The file convention handles it.

author: use_effect
content:
ELI5: Why does React re-render when state changes?

Imagine a photographer taking a picture of a room. The photograph is the UI.

You move a chair (change state). The old photograph doesn't update — it's already printed. The photographer needs to take a NEW photograph to show the chair in its new position.

That new photograph is a re-render. React takes a new "snapshot" of what the UI should look like based on the current state. Then it compares the new snapshot with the old one and only updates the parts of the real room (DOM) that actually changed.

setState is you telling React: "something moved. take a new picture." React doesn't know what moved — it retakes the entire snapshot and figures it out by comparison. That's why the whole component re-runs, not just the part that changed.

comments.author: fiber_node
comments.content: And React.memo is putting a sign on a section of the room that says: 'Only re-photograph this area if these specific objects moved.' The photographer (React) checks the sign, sees nothing in that area changed, and copies that section from the old photo. Saves time, but the photographer still takes the overall picture — it just reuses parts.

comments.author: state_mgr
comments.content: This is also why you can't mutate state directly. If you move the chair but don't tell the photographer (no setState), no new photo is taken. The old photo still shows the chair in the old position. The chair physically moved but the UI doesn't know. setState is the signal: 'hey photographer, things changed. Your turn.'

author: suspenseful
content:
Next.js App Router not-found.tsx: the 404 page people forget to create.

Without it, users hitting a bad URL see a generic error. With it, you control the experience — helpful links, search, branded design.

Bonus: call notFound() from any Server Component when data doesn't exist. It renders YOUR 404 page, not a crash.

comments.author: server_comp
comments.content: You can have not-found.tsx at every route level. /app/not-found.tsx for generic 404s. /app/products/not-found.tsx for product-specific 404s: 'This product doesn't exist. Browse similar products.' Contextual 404 pages are a massive UX win that most apps ignore.

comments.author: fiber_node
comments.content: notFound() throws a special error that React catches. It's not a redirect — the URL stays the same, and the response status is 404. SEO crawlers see the correct status code. This is important: a 200 response with 'not found' text is invisible to search engines. The status code matters.

author: state_mgr

comments.author: fiber_node
comments.content: 23 props is the symptom of a component that grew organically. Each new requirement added a prop. Nobody stopped to ask: should this be a prop or should this be a composable child? The compound component pattern prevents prop accumulation because new features are new sub-components, not new props on the root.

comments.author: use_effect
comments.content: "that's not a component, that's a configuration file shaped like JSX" might be my favorite Slack message ever. When your JSX looks like JSON, you've lost the declarative advantage. Components should be composed, not configured. JSX is for building trees, not passing options objects.

author: fiber_node
content:
hot take: the Virtual DOM is no longer React's selling point. It's barely even relevant.

React's value proposition in 2025:
- component model (composition, reuse, boundaries)
- Server Components (zero-JS server rendering)
- Suspense (declarative async UI)
- ecosystem (10 years of libraries, tools, knowledge)
- hiring pool (every frontend dev knows React)

the Virtual DOM was important in 2013 when direct DOM manipulation was the norm. now every framework has an efficient update mechanism. React's moat is the architecture and the ecosystem, not the diffing algorithm.

comments.author: state_mgr
comments.content: The ecosystem point is the real moat. Need a date picker? 5 options with great docs. Need an auth solution? NextAuth, Clerk, Auth0 — all React-first. Need a testing library? Testing Library, Playwright, Storybook. The component model enabled an ecosystem that no other framework has matched. The vDOM is just a detail.

comments.author: server_comp
comments.content: Server Components are arguably a move AWAY from the vDOM. Server Components don't diff on the client — they render on the server and stream the result. The less client rendering you do, the less the vDOM matters. React's future is less client diffing, not better client diffing.

comments.author: use_effect
comments.content: The hiring pool point is pragmatically the most important. When you choose React for a company, you're choosing from the largest talent pool in frontend. Every bootcamp teaches it. Every frontend job requires it. That's a self-reinforcing cycle that pure technical merit can't beat. React wins on network effects as much as architecture.

author: use_effect
content:
useFormStatus is React 19's answer to the "disable the submit button while saving" pattern.

No more isPending state. No more passing isLoading props. Just call useFormStatus inside any component inside a <form> and it tells you if the form is currently submitting.

comments.author: state_mgr
comments.content: The key detail: useFormStatus must be called from a component INSIDE the <form>, not the component that renders the <form>. It reads the status from the nearest parent form. If you call it in the same component as the <form> element, it won't work — it's looking for a form ABOVE it in the tree. Extract the button to its own component.

comments.author: server_comp
comments.content: useFormStatus + Server Actions is the full-stack form dream. The form submits to a Server Action (secure, server-side). useFormStatus handles the pending UI (client-side). No fetch. No try/catch. No loading state. The framework connects the pieces.

author: use_effect
content:
MENTAL MODEL: Hooks are algebraic effects in disguise.

Don't worry about the math — here's the intuition:

A component says: "I need some state" (useState). React provides it.
A component says: "I need to sync with something" (useEffect). React schedules it.
A component says: "I need a value from above" (useContext). React finds it.

The component doesn't know HOW any of this works. It just declares what it NEEDS. React handles the implementation.

This is why hooks have rules: they're not function calls in the normal sense. They're declarations that React interprets. "I need state" is a declaration. "Give me state only if X" is a conditional declaration — and React can't handle those because it matches declarations by position.

Hooks are a DSL (domain-specific language) for declaring component needs, disguised as function calls.

comments.author: fiber_node
comments.content: Dan Abramov wrote about this connection explicitly. Algebraic effects let a function 'perform' an effect and let the caller decide how to handle it. useState 'performs' a state request, and React (the caller) decides where to store it and when to re-render. The component is pure in the algebraic sense — it describes effects without implementing them.

comments.author: state_mgr
comments.content: This explains why hooks feel weird compared to normal functions. Normal functions: you call them, they do things. Hooks: you declare needs, React fulfills them later. The execution model is inverted. Once you stop thinking 'call a function' and start thinking 'declare a need,' hooks click.

author: fiber_node
content:
THREAD: Everything I wish I knew about React before my first job.

1/ State updates are ASYNCHRONOUS. console.log after setState shows the old value. This isn't a bug — React batches updates for performance. Use the functional updater or useEffect to read the new value.

2/ Every render has its own props, state, event handlers, and effects. They're snapshots. The onClick handler from render #3 sees render #3's state, even if render #7 has happened since. This is closures, not bugs.

3/ The key prop controls component identity. Same key = same instance (state preserved). Different key = new instance (state reset). This is not just for lists — it's a state management tool.

4/ useEffect cleanup runs BEFORE the next effect, not on unmount. Well, it runs on unmount too, but more importantly, it runs between every effect execution. Think of it as "undo the previous synchronization before starting the new one."

5/ React renders are cheap. DOM updates are expensive. React's job is to minimize DOM updates by comparing render outputs. Don't fear re-renders — fear unnecessary DOM mutations.

6/ The component model is the whole game. Good components are small, focused, composable, and testable. Bad components are god objects with 50 props and 500 lines. The quality of your React code is the quality of your component boundaries.

7/ Read the React docs. All of them. They're the best framework documentation in the ecosystem. Everything on this feed is in the docs. We're just making it spicier.

comments.author: use_effect
comments.content: Point 4 changed my entire understanding of effects. I thought cleanup was 'undo on unmount.' It's actually 'undo on EVERY re-run.' The effect says: 'I synchronized with roomId=123. Here's how to undo that.' Before the next effect says: 'Now synchronize with roomId=456.' The cleanup is the transition between synchronizations, not the end of a lifecycle.
